# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List and briefly explain any FOUR major application domains of IoT.**

**Model Answer (Verbatim Board Style):**

1. **Smart Home:** Integration of household devices (lighting, HVAC, security, entertainment) onto a single IP-based network for remote and automated control. Typical protocols include Wi-Fi, ZigBee, and BLE.
2. **Smart Healthcare:** Wearable and implantable sensors (ECG, SpO2, glucose monitors) transmit patient vitals over BLE / 5G to cloud platforms for continuous remote monitoring and predictive diagnostics.
3. **Smart Agriculture:** Soil, weather, and crop sensors relay data over LoRaWAN to cloud ML pipelines that drive precision irrigation, fertilization, and yield forecasting.
4. **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Vibration, current, and temperature sensors on machines feed ML-based predictive maintenance systems, reducing unplanned downtime.

*(Any four from the KTU-approved domain list fetch full 3 marks.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between mMTC, eMBB, and URLLC as IoT service classes of 5G.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Service Class** | **Full Form** | **Target Use-Case** | **Data Rate** | **Latency** | **Device Density** |
|-------------------|---------------|---------------------|---------------|-------------|--------------------|
| mMTC | Massive Machine-Type Communication | Smart metering, agriculture | Low (< 1 Mbps) | High (seconds) | $10^6$ / km² |
| eMBB | Enhanced Mobile BroadBand | Video surveillance, AR/VR | Very High (1–10 Gbps) | Moderate (10 ms) | Moderate |
| URLLC | Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Comm. | V2X, robotic control, IIoT | Medium | < 1 ms | Low |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** Each Part-B carries 14 marks, split typically as 7 + 7 in sub-parts (a) and (b).

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **smart city** as an IoT application. List its sub-systems, the sensors/devices used, the communication technologies, and the data flow from field to dashboard.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A smart-parking deployment in a city has **1,000 parking slots**. Each slot is fitted with an ultrasonic sensor that sends a **16-byte** status packet (occupied/free) every **5 minutes** to the cloud. The cloud runs a recommendation engine and pushes a **32-byte** routing message to driver mobile apps.
Compute:
(i) The **daily uplink data volume** in MB.
(ii) The **monthly downlink volume** in GB, assuming 30,000 app users receive 1 routing message/day.
(iii) Recommend the most suitable **LPWAN** technology and justify using two performance metrics.

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Smart City IoT — Detailed Explanation (7 marks)**

A **Smart City** uses IoT to deliver citizen-centric services with data-driven governance. Major **sub-systems** (1 mark each for first four, 3 marks for the data-flow explanation):

1. **Smart Lighting** — Streetlights with embedded LUX + motion sensors and LED dimming actuators, connected over LoRa/NB-IoT to a city lighting CMS, achieving 40–60% energy savings.
2. **Smart Parking** — Ultrasonic / magnetic sensors on each slot, uplink to cloud; mobile app shows real-time availability. (Often a KTU-favourite example.)
3. **Smart Waste Management** — Ultrasonic fill-level sensors in bins, GPS-tracked collection trucks, dynamic route optimization.
4. **Environmental Monitoring** — Air-quality (PM2.5, CO₂, NO₂) and noise sensors deployed across the city, public dashboard, AQI alerts.
5. **Smart Water & Gas Metering** — Automated meter reading (AMR/AMI) over NB-IoT.

**Data Flow (3 marks):** Sensor → Edge Gateway (e.g., LoRaWAN GW / 5G gNB) → City Data Lake (Kafka/TimeSeries DB) → Analytics (ML, GIS) → Citizen Apps + City Command Centre. Security: TLS/DTLS, OAuth2, blockchain for tamper-proof records.

**(b) Smart-Parking Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $N = 1000$ sensors.
- $D_{\text{up}} = 16$ bytes per packet.
- $f = \frac{60}{5} = 12$ packets/hour.
- $T = 24$ hours.

**(i) Daily Uplink Volume:**

$$ V_{\text{up/day}} = 1000 \cdot 16 \cdot 12 \cdot 24 $$

$$ V_{\text{up/day}} = 1000 \cdot 16 \cdot 288 $$

$$ V_{\text{up/day}} = 4{,}608{,}000\ \text{bytes/day} $$

$$ V_{\text{up/day (MB)}} = \frac{4{,}608{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 4.395\ \text{MB/day} $$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Monthly Downlink Volume (30,000 users × 32 bytes/day):**

$$ V_{\text{down/day}} = 30{,}000 \cdot 32 = 960{,}000\ \text{bytes/day} $$

$$ V_{\text{down/month (bytes)}} = 960{,}000 \cdot 30 = 28{,}800{,}000\ \text{bytes} $$

$$ V_{\text{down/month (GB)}} = \frac{28{,}800{,}000}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824} \approx 0.0268\ \text{GB/month} $$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Recommended LPWAN Technology (3 marks):**

- **Recommended: LoRaWAN** (or NB-IoT as alternative).
- **Justification — Metric 1 (Range):** LoRaWAN provides **2–5 km urban** and **15 km rural** range, sufficient for city-wide slot deployment without dense gateway infrastructure.
- **Justification — Metric 2 (Battery Life):** LoRaWAN nodes consume < **1%** duty cycle, yielding > **10-year** battery life on a 2400 mAh cell, ideal for inaccessible underground slots.
- **Bonus (1 mark):** LoRaWAN's low cost (~ \$3–5 per module) makes scaling to 1,000 sensors economically viable.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 — Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a **neat architecture diagram**, explain an **IoT-based remote patient monitoring (RPM) system for elderly patients**. Cover the sensing, communication, edge analytics, and emergency-response layers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wearable health patch operates with these parameters:
- Active current: $30\ \text{mA}$ for $0.2\ \text{s}$ every minute.
- TX current: $80\ \text{mA}$ for $0.4\ \text{s}$ every minute.
- Sleep current: $5\ \mu\text{A}$ for the rest of the minute.
- Battery: $400\ \text{mAh}$.

Compute the **average current** and **battery life in days**.

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) IoT-based RPM System (7 marks)**

**Architecture (3 marks for diagram + 4 marks for explanation):**

- **Sensing Layer:** Wearable patch with ECG (ADS1292), SpO2 (MAX30102), body temperature (MAX30205), and 3-axis accelerometer (ADXL345).
- **Communication Layer:** BLE 5.0 from patch to a **smartphone gateway** (5 m range, low power); the smartphone uses 4G/5G/Wi-Fi for **backhaul** to the cloud.
- **Edge Analytics:** On-phone ML (TensorFlow Lite) detects anomalies (e.g., arrhythmia, fall detection) and triggers local alerts.
- **Cloud Layer:** Time-series DB (InfluxDB), ML model for long-term risk scoring, FHIR-compliant EMR integration.
- **Emergency Response:** Geo-fenced alerts to ambulance services, caregivers, and primary physician on critical events.
- **Security & Privacy:** HIPAA / DISHA compliance, AES-128 payload encryption, mutual TLS to the cloud.

**(b) Battery-Life Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute average currents per hour for each mode.**

For a 1-minute window (60 s):
- $T_{\text{active}} = 0.2\ \text{s}$.
- $T_{\text{tx}} = 0.4\ \text{s}$.
- $T_{\text{sleep}} = 60 - 0.6 = 59.4\ \text{s}$.

**Step 2 — Time-averaged current (per minute, in mA).**

$$ I_{\text{active (avg)}} = 30 \cdot \frac{0.2}{60} = 0.1000\ \text{mA} $$

$$ I_{\text{tx (avg)}} = 80 \cdot \frac{0.4}{60} = 0.5333\ \text{mA} $$

$$ I_{\text{sleep (avg)}} = 0.005 \cdot \frac{59.4}{60} = 0.00495\ \text{mA} $$

**Step 3 — Total average current.**

$$ I_{\text{avg}} = 0.1000 + 0.5333 + 0.00495 $$

$$ I_{\text{avg}} \approx 0.638\ \text{mA} $$

**(2 marks for step 2 & 3)**

**Step 4 — Battery life in hours.**

$$ \text{Life (h)} = \frac{400\ \text{mAh}}{0.638\ \text{mA}} \approx 627.0\ \text{hours} $$

**Step 5 — Convert to days.**

$$ \text{Life (days)} = \frac{627.0}{24} \approx 26.1\ \text{days} $$

**(2 marks for steps 4 & 5)**

**Step 6 — Engineering Conclusion (3 marks).**
> A **~ 26-day** battery life is acceptable for short-term clinical patches. For long-term elderly monitoring, KTU expects recommendations such as:
> - Use **energy harvesting** (body-heat thermoelectric + photovoltaic).
> - Reduce **TX duty cycle** via data compression and adaptive sampling.
> - Use a **higher-capacity flexible Li-Po battery** (1000 mAh) to push life beyond 60 days.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't confuse application with technology.** KTU deducts marks if you describe "Wi-Fi" or "5G" *as* an application — they are **enabling technologies**. Always begin with the **vertical domain** first.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** is the #1 reason for losing 1–2 marks. Always carry units (mA, mAh, MB, GB) through every step.
> 3. **Loose terminology** between *mMTC*, *eMBB*, *URLLC* and *LPWAN*, *PAN*, *BAN* — board examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping a labelled architecture diagram** in 7-mark sub-parts costs you at least 2 marks. Always draw the 4-layer IoT stack: Sensing → Network → Cloud/Edge → Application.
> 5. **Recommending the wrong protocol** for a numerical: e.g., suggesting 5G for a 4 MB/day smart-parking system loses 1 mark — the correct answer is LoRaWAN/NB-IoT.
> 6. **Forgetting the monthly/annual dimension:** KTU Part-B numericals almost always ask you to scale daily → monthly → yearly. Show every conversion step.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — IoT Applications**

- **Definition:** IoT applications = vertical-specific cyber-physical systems combining sensing, communication, analytics, and actuation.
- **Mandatory KTU Domains:** Smart Home, Smart City, Smart Healthcare, Smart Agriculture, **IIoT**, Smart Energy, Smart Logistics, Smart Retail, Smart Environment.
- **5G Service Classes:** **mMTC** (massive density), **eMBB** (high bandwidth), **URLLC** (low latency + reliability).
- **End-to-End Latency Formula:**
  $$ T_{\text{e2e}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{process}} + T_{\text{transmit}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{actuate}} $$
- **Real-time IIoT bound:** $T_{\text{e2e}} \le \mathbf{10\ \text{ms}}$.
- **Data-Volume Formula:**
  $$ V = N \cdot D \cdot f \cdot T $$
  Always convert bytes → KB (÷1024) → MB (÷1024²) → GB (÷1024³).
- **Battery-Life Formula:**
  $$ \text{Life (years)} = \frac{C_{\text{battery}}}{I_{\text{avg}} \cdot 8760} $$
- **Sleep current dominates** low-duty-cycle IoT — design for $< \mathbf{10\ \mu A}$ sleep current.
- **Protocol Selection Heuristic:**
  - Low data + long range + battery → **LoRaWAN / NB-IoT / Sigfox**.
  - High data + short range + line-power → **Wi-Fi 6 / Ethernet**.
  - Ultra-low-latency control → **5G URLLC / TSN**.
  - Body-area / wearables → **BLE 5.0**.
- **Reference Architectures (must memorize):** 4-layer IoT stack — *Perception → Network → Edge/Cloud → Application*.
- **Reliability Benchmark:** Industrial IoT = **99.999%** (five nines).
- **Device-Density Benchmark:** 5G mMTC supports $10^6$ devices/km².
- **Energy-Harvesting Options (viva favourite):** Solar, vibrational piezoelectric, thermal (TEG), RF harvesting.
- **Security Stack (frequently tested):** AES-128 (data), TLS/DTLS (transport), OAuth2 (auth), blockchain (tamper-proofing).
- **Smart-City Reference KPIs:** 30–50% energy saving, 40% water saving, 70% downtime reduction in IIoT.
- **Key Differentiators to Write in Answers:** Always link the **application requirement → protocol choice → performance metric**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Future Internet Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT612 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Future Internet Technologies # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Defining the "Future Internet" > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition** > The **Future Internet** refers to a collection of emerging networking paradigms, protocols, and architectural frameworks designed to overcome the inherent limitations of the current TCP/IP-based Internet (IPv4, rigid routing, host-centric addressing) in order to natively support the explosive growth of **IoT devices**, **mobile users**, **content distribution**, and **machine-to-machine (M2M) communication**. The current Internet was conceived in the **1970s** with four hosts, primarily for *host-to-host* file transfer. Today it connects **billions** of devices — most of which are sensors, actuators, and smart objects — and the original architecture is straining at the seams. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)** > Students must be able to explain the *limitations of the present Internet*, the role of **IPv6**, **Mobile IPv6**, the transition toward **Software Defined Networking (SDN)** and **Named Data Networking (NDN/CCN)**, and how these collectively enable the Future Internet vision that IoT depends upon. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine the **current Internet** as an old **postal system**: | Old Postal System (Today's Internet) | Modern Courier Network (Future Internet) | |---|---| | You must know the exact house number (IP address) to send a letter. | You just say *"deliver the package containing this content"* — the network finds it. | | The postman follows a fixed route decided by a central post office (traditional routing). | Each courier dynamically chooses the fastest path (SDN-controlled, programmable). | | If the house moves, mail is lost (mobility problem). | The package is forwarded automatically to the new address (Mobile IPv6 / NDN mobility). | | Only ~4.3 billion house numbers exist (IPv4). | Practically unlimited addresses (IPv6: **2¹²⁸**). | So when we say **"Future Internet Technologies,"** we mean the *modernization of the postal system* — making it content-aware, programmable, mobile-friendly, and virtually infinite in addressing capacity. --- ## 1.3 Key Future Internet Pillars (KTU Taxonomy) > [!NOTE] > **The Five Pillars of the Future Internet (as per KTU 2024 Module 1)** 1. **IPv6 & Mobile IPv6** — Solving address exhaustion and mobility. 2. **Software Defined Networking (SDN)** — Decoupling control from data plane. 3. **Network Function Virtualization (NFV)** — Replacing hardware with software. 4. **Named Data Networking (NDN) / Content-Centric Networking (CCN)** — Content-first, not host-first. 5. **Edge / Fog Computing & 5G** — Pushing computation closer to data sources (crucial for IoT). --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **IPv4 Address Space** | **2³² = 4,294,967,296** | Already exhausted (2011, IANA). | | **IPv6 Address Space** | **2¹²⁸ ≈ 3.4 × 10³⁸** | Effectively unlimited. | | **MTU for IPv6** | **≥ 1280 bytes** (recommended 1500) | Minimum transmission unit. | | **5G Peak Data Rate** | **10 Gbps** | Versus 4G's 1 Gbps theoretical. | | **5G Latency Target** | **< 1 ms (URLLC)** | Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication. | | **IoT Devices by 2030 (Cisco Est.)** | **> 29.3 billion** | Drives Future Internet demand. | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Pillar I — IPv6: The Address Exhaustion Fix ### 2.1.1 Why IPv4 Failed The **32-bit** IPv4 space yields only **4.29 billion** addresses. With mobile phones, IoT sensors, smart cars, and embedded devices each needing a unique address, the space was exhausted by **February 2011** (IANA exhaustion). ### 2.1.2 IPv6 Header Format IPv6 simplifies processing by using a **fixed 40-byte header** with **8 fields**, eliminating IPv4's variable options and checksum: ``` +-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+ | Version | Traffic | Flow Label | | (4 bits) | Class(8) | (20 bits) | +-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+ | Payload Length (16) | Next Hdr (8) | Hop(8)| +-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+ | Source Address (128 bits) | +----------------------------------------------------+ | Destination Address (128 bits) | +----------------------------------------------------+ ``` ### 2.1.3 Operational Advantages - **Hierarchical addressing & routing** — efficient aggregation. - **Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (SLAAC)** — no DHCP needed. - **Built-in IPsec** — security by design. - **No broadcast** — replaced by multicast (saves bandwidth). - **Extension headers** — kept out of the main header for speed. ### 2.1.4 The KTU Formula Sheet | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | Total IPv4 addresses | $N_{v4} = 2^{32}$ | addresses | Proves exhaustion | | Total IPv6 addresses | $N_{v6} = 2^{128}$ | addresses | Proves scalability | | IPv6 header overhead | $H_{v6} = 40$ | bytes | QoS/bandwidth calc | | Address density per m² on Earth | $\rho = \frac{2^{128}}{5.1 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^2}$ | addresses/m² | Shows practical infinity | | Compression ratio (abbrev. IPv6) | $C_r = \frac{128 \cdot 4}{L_{\text{rep}}} $ | — | Routing efficiency | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** You will not be asked to derive $2^{128}$, but you **must** be able to compare $2^{32}$ vs $2^{128}$ and explain *why* IoT demands IPv6. Memorize **40-byte header** and **128-bit address**. --- ## 2.2 Pillar II — Mobile IPv6 (MIPv6) > [!NOTE] > **Definition** > **Mobile IPv6** is a protocol that allows an IPv6 node to **remain reachable** at its *permanent home address* even while roaming across foreign networks, by using a **Care-of Address (CoA)** and **Binding Updates** sent to the Home Agent. ### 2.2.1 Three Critical Entities 1. **Mobile Node (MN)** — The moving device (e.g., a smartphone in a moving car). 2. **Home Agent (HA)** — Router at the MN's home network that tunnels traffic. 3. **Correspondent Node (CN)** — The peer the MN is communicating with (a server, another user). ### 2.2.2 Operational Steps - **Step 1:** MN attaches to a foreign network → obtains **CoA** via IPv6 autoconfiguration. - **Step 2:** MN sends a **Binding Update (BU)** to its Home Agent, registering the CoA. - **Step 3:** HA intercepts packets destined to MN's home address and **tunnels** them to the CoA. - **Step 4:** MN replies *directly* to CN using **Route Optimization** (avoids triangular routing). ### 2.2.3 Why MIPv6 Matters for IoT Vehicular IoT, drone swarms, wearable health monitors — all are **mobile IoT nodes**. Without MIPv6, ongoing TCP sessions would break on every handover. --- ## 2.3 Pillar III — Software Defined Networking (SDN) > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Standard)** > **SDN** is a networking architecture that **decouples the control plane** (deciding where traffic goes) **from the data plane** (forwarding packets), centralizing network intelligence in a software-based **SDN Controller** that communicates with switches via standardized APIs (e.g., **OpenFlow**). ### 2.3.1 The Three-Layer SDN Architecture | Layer | Function | Example | |---|---|---| | **Application Layer** | Business logic, routing apps, firewalls | Load balancer, intrusion detection | | **Control Layer** | SDN Controller — brain of the network | OpenDaylight, ONOS, Ryu | | **Infrastructure Layer** | Physical/virtual switches & routers | OpenFlow switches | ### 2.3.2 OpenFlow — The Southbound API OpenFlow defines a **flow table** in each switch. The controller installs/modifies flow entries: ``` +--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+ | Match | Priority| Counters| Actions| Time- | | Fields | | | |outs | +--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+ ``` A *match-action* paradigm: controller says *"match this 5-tuple, forward it out port 3"* → switch obeys. ### 2.3.3 Engineering Utility For IoT, SDN allows **dynamic QoS provisioning** — e.g., a smart hospital can prioritize *life-critical* heart-monitor traffic over *non-critical* HVAC telemetry, all in **real time** without touching physical switches. --- ## 2.4 Pillar IV — Network Function Virtualization (NFV) > [!NOTE] > **Definition** > **NFV** virtualizes entire **classes of network node functions** (firewalls, load balancers, NAT, DPI) into software modules called **Virtual Network Functions (VNFs)** that run on commodity **x86 servers**, replacing proprietary hardware appliances. ### 2.4.1 NFV Reference Architecture (ETSI) ``` +--------+ +--------+ +--------+ | VNFs | -> | NFVI | -> |MANO/OSS| | (fire- | |(Compute| |(Orchest| | wall) | |Storage | | ration)| +--------+ |Network)| +--------+ +--------+ ``` ### 2.4.2 SDN vs NFV — The Classic KTU Distinction | Aspect | SDN | NFV | |---|---|---| | **Goal** | Separate control from data plane | Decouple network functions from hardware | | **Scope** | Routing/forwarding | Entire network services (firewall, DNS, etc.) | | **Relationship** | Can be used **with** NFV | Can be used **with** SDN | | **Standard Body** | ONF | ETSI | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"How are SDN and NFV related but different?"* — The answer above is worth **7 marks** in a typical ESE question. --- ## 2.5 Pillar V — Named Data Networking (NDN) / Content-Centric Networking (CCN) > [!NOTE] > **Definition** > **NDN** (formerly **Content-Centric Networking**) is a Future Internet architecture where communication is driven by **what** the user wants (the *named content*), not **where** it lives (the *IP address*). It uses two packet types: **Interest** and **Data**. ### 2.5.1 Core Idea In NDN, a user does **not** request `http://203.0.113.45/video.mp4`. Instead, the user sends an **Interest packet** carrying the name `/kerala/news/video.mp4`. Any router in the path that has a cached copy responds with the **Data packet**. ### 2.5.2 The Three Data Structures in Every NDN Router | Structure | Purpose | |---|---| | **Content Store (CS)** | Cache of recently forwarded Data packets (like a CDN). | | **Pending Interest Table (PIT)** | Records Interests not yet satisfied, so returning Data can be forwarded back. | | **Forwarding Information Base (FIB)** | Routing table populated by name-prefix-based protocols (e.g., NLSR). | ### 2.5.3 The Magic of Built-in Mobility If the producer moves, the Interest is just **re-issued** from a new path. No binding updates, no home agents — mobility is *free* because we never pinned content to a location. --- ## 2.6 Quick-Reference KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet | Concept | Key Number / Term | Marks Worth | |---|---|---| | IPv4 address length | **32 bits** | 2 | | IPv6 address length | **128 bits** | 2 | | IPv6 fixed header size | **40 bytes** | 2 | | IPv6 min MTU | **1280 bytes** | 1 | | Mobile IPv6 main protocol msg | **Binding Update (BU)** | 3 | | SDN southbound API | **OpenFlow** | 4 | | SDN Controller examples | **OpenDaylight, ONOS, Ryu, POX** | 2 | | NFV standards body | **ETSI** | 2 | | NDN packet types | **Interest, Data** | 3 | | NDN router structures | **CS, PIT, FIB** | 4 | | 5G eMBB peak rate | **10 Gbps** | 1 | | 5G URLLC latency | **< 1 ms** | 1 | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Quantitative Comparison of IPv4 vs IPv6 The KTU examiner loves a numerical proof. Let's derive why IPv6 is "infinite" in practical terms. ### 3.1.1 Total Address Count We start with the fundamental relationship that an address of $b$ bits can represent $2^b$ unique values. $$ N_{\text{IPv4}} = 2^{32} $$ $$ N_{\text{IPv4}} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \;\; \text{addresses} $$ $$ N_{\text{IPv6}} = 2^{128} $$ ### 3.1.2 The Multiplicative Scaling Factor To find how many times larger IPv6 is, we compute the ratio: $$ R = \frac{N_{\text{IPv6}}}{N_{\text{IPv4}}} = \frac{2^{128}}{2^{32}} $$ Using the law of exponents $\frac{a^m}{a^n} = a^{m-n}$: $$ R = 2^{128 - 32} = 2^{96} $$ Therefore: $$ R = 2^{96} \approx 7.92 \times 10^{28} $$ > **Interpretation:** IPv6 provides roughly **79 octillion** times more addresses than IPv4 — a number larger than the estimated number of atoms in a human body multiplied by a billion. ### 3.1.3 Practical Density Check We divide the IPv6 space by Earth's land surface area (≈ 1.49 × 10¹⁴ m², including land) to show real density. $$ \rho = \frac{2^{128}}{1.49 \times 10^{14}} \;\; \text{addresses/m}^2 $$ $$ \rho \approx 2.28 \times 10^{24} \;\; \text{addresses/m}^2 $$ > **Conclusion:** Every square meter of Earth's land can have **2.28 × 10²⁴** IPv6 addresses — making address exhaustion a *non-problem* for any conceivable IoT deployment scale. --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — Mobile IPv6 Handover Latency Approximation The handover delay in MIPv6 is dominated by three sequential operations. A KTU numerical/derivation problem may ask to compute the total delay. $$ D_{\text{total}} = D_{\text{detect}} + D_{\text{coacq}} + D_{\text{BU}} $$ Where: - $D_{\text{detect}}$ = link-layer detection time (router advertisement delay). - $D_{\text{coacq}}$ = time to obtain a new Care-of Address (Duplicate Address Detection). - $D_{\text{BU}}$ = round-trip time for the Binding Update to reach the Home Agent. ### 3.2.1 Worked Numerical Example Suppose a sensor moves between routers in a smart factory. The following values are measured: $$ D_{\text{detect}} = 50 \text{ ms} $$ $$ D_{\text{coacq}} = 1000 \text{ ms} \;\; \text{(typical for DAD)} $$ $$ D_{\text{BU}} = 2 \cdot t_{\text{prop}} = 2 \cdot 25 = 50 \text{ ms} $$ Then: $$ D_{\text{total}} = 50 + 1000 + 50 $$ $$ D_{\text{total}} = 1100 \text{ ms} = 1.1 \text{ s} $$ > **Engineering Insight:** Such latency is **unacceptable** for URLLC IoT (e.g., autonomous vehicles needing < 1 ms). This is why **5G + edge computing + Proxy MIPv6 (PMIPv6)** are researched in Future Internet. ### 3.2.2 Optimized Fast Handover (FMIPv6) To reduce the above, FMIPv6 pre-acquires the CoA *before* the actual link break, so $D_{\text{coacq}}$ can be reduced to: $$ D_{\text{coacq}}^{\text{fast}} \approx 0 \;\; \text{(pre-computed)} $$ Hence: $$ D_{\text{total}}^{\text{FMIPv6}} = D_{\text{detect}} + D_{\text{BU}} = 50 + 50 = 100 \text{ ms} $$ A **11×** improvement — exactly the kind of analysis a KTU 14-mark question demands. --- ## 3.3 Derivation III — SDN Flow Table Lookup Complexity SDN switches classify packets using multi-field matching. A naive lookup is **O(N)** in the number of flow entries. KTU may ask to compare this with traditional LPM (Longest Prefix Match) lookup. ### 3.3.1 Traditional LPM Trie Complexity A binary trie of depth $W$ (where $W = 32$ for IPv4) has lookup: $$ T_{\text{LPM}} = O(W) = O(32) $$ ### 3.3.2 SDN OpenFlow TCAM Lookup Modern switches use **Ternary Content Addressable Memory (TCAM)**, which is *parallel* across all entries: $$ T_{\text{TCAM}} = O(1) $$ > **Conclusion:** SDN switches make per-packet forwarding decisions in *constant time*, enabling **terabit-scale** IoT aggregation backbones. ### 3.3.3 Trade-off Table | Memory | Speed | Cost | Power | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **TCAM** | **O(1)** | High | High | SDN/OpenFlow core | | **SRAM + Trie** | O(W) | Low | Low | Software routers | | **DRAM** | O(N) | Very Low | Lowest | CPU-based forwarding | --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Future Internet Trade-offs | Technology | Strength | Weakness | IoT Fit | |---|---|---|---| | **IPv6** | Infinite addresses, security | Migration cost, NAT legacy | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Mobile IPv6** | Session continuity | Triangle routing, HA bottleneck | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **SDN** | Programmability, central control | Single point of failure (controller) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **NFV** | Hardware cost reduction | VNF chaining overhead | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **NDN/CCN** | Native caching, inherent mobility | Routing scalability (name explosion) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **5G/Edge** | Low latency, high bandwidth | Spectrum cost, dense cells | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Future Internet Technology Stack — High-Level View ```mermaid flowchart TB appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER
IoT Apps, Smart City, Health, Industry 4.0"] ndnLayer["NAMED DATA NETWORKING LAYER
Interest / Data Packets - CS PIT FIB"] sdnCtrl["SDN CONTROL PLANE
OpenDaylight - ONOS - Ryu"] nfvLayer["NFV INFRASTRUCTURE
VNFs - Firewalls - DPI - vCPE"] netLayer["NETWORK LAYER
IPv6 - Mobile IPv6 - 5G"] edgeLayer["EDGE / FOG COMPUTING
MEC Servers - Cloudlets"] physLayer["PHYSICAL LAYER
Sensors - Actuators - RF - Optical"] appLayer --> ndnLayer ndnLayer --> sdnCtrl sdnCtrl --> nfvLayer nfvLayer --> netLayer netLayer --> edgeLayer edgeLayer --> physLayer sdnCtrl -.->|"Southbound API
OpenFlow"| netLayer nfvLayer -.->|"MANO Orchestration"| edgeLayer ``` --- ## 4.2 Mobile IPv6 Handover Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant MN as Mobile Node participant FA1 as Foreign Agent 1 participant HA as Home Agent participant CN as Correspondent Node Note over MN: Connected to Home Network MN->>HA: Initial Binding Registration HA-->>CN: Tunnel traffic to MN Home Address Note over MN: MN moves to FA1 FA1->>MN: Router Advertisement MN->>FA1: Acquire Care-of Address CoA1 MN->>HA: Binding Update with CoA1 HA-->>HA: Update binding cache HA->>CN: Route Optimization - direct delivery Note over MN: MN moves to FA2 FA2->>MN: New Router Advertisement MN->>FA2: Acquire CoA2 MN->>HA: Binding Update with CoA2 HA-->>CN: Forward via new CoA2 ``` --- ## 4.3 SDN Architecture — Three-Layer Functional View ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER"] A1[Load Balancer] A2[Firewall App] A3[Routing Policy] end subgraph CTRL["CONTROL LAYER - SDN CONTROLLER"] C1[OpenDaylight] C2[ONOS] C3[Ryu] end subgraph INFRA["INFRASTRUCTURE LAYER - DATA PLANE"] S1[OpenFlow Switch 1] S2[OpenFlow Switch 2] S3[OpenFlow Switch 3] end A1 --> C1 A2 --> C2 A3 --> C3 C1 -->|OpenFlow| S1 C2 -->|OpenFlow| S2 C3 -->|OpenFlow| S3 S1 -.->|Flow packets| S2 S2 -.->|Flow packets| S3 ``` --- ## 4.4 NDN — Interest / Data Forwarding Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD user["USER / CONSUMER"] r1["NDN ROUTER 1
CS PIT FIB"] r2["NDN ROUTER 2
CS PIT FIB"] r3["NDN ROUTER 3
CS PIT FIB"] prod["DATA PRODUCER
Original Source"] user -->|"Interest /video/kerala.mp4"| r1 r1 -->|"Interest /video/kerala.mp4"| r2 r2 -->|"Interest /video/kerala.mp4"| r3 r3 -->|"Interest /video/kerala.mp4"| prod prod -->|"Data /video/kerala.mp4"| r3 r3 -->|"Data /video/kerala.mp4"| r2 r2 -->|"Data /video/kerala.mp4"| r1 r1 -->|"Data /video/kerala.mp4"| user r3 -.->|"Caches Data in CS"| r3 r2 -.->|"Caches Data in CS"| r2 ``` --- ## 4.5 IPv4 vs IPv6 Header Comparison Block ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph V4["IPv4 HEADER - Variable 20 to 60 bytes"] v4_1[Version 4 bits] v4_2[IHL 4 bits] v4_3[TOS 8 bits] v4_4[Total Length 16 bits] v4_5[ID 16 bits] v4_6[Flags 3 bits] v4_7[Fragment Offset 13 bits] v4_8[TTL 8 bits] v4_9[Protocol 8 bits] v4_10[Header Checksum 16 bits] v4_11[Source IP 32 bits] v4_12[Dest IP 32 bits] v4_13[Options 0 to 40 bytes] end subgraph V6["IPv6 HEADER - FIXED 40 bytes"] v6_1[Version 4 bits] v6_2[Traffic Class 8 bits] v6_3[Flow Label 20 bits] v6_4[Payload Length 16 bits] v6_5[Next Header 8 bits] v6_6[Hop Limit 8 bits] v6_7[Source Address 128 bits] v6_8[Destination Address 128 bits] end ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] *List any three advantages of IPv6 over IPv4.* **Model Answer:** 1. **Larger Address Space:** IPv6 uses **128-bit** addresses ($2^{128}$ total) versus IPv4's 32-bit ($2^{32}$), effectively eliminating address exhaustion — critical for IoT scale. 2. **Simplified Header:** IPv6 has a **fixed 40-byte header** (vs. variable 20–60 bytes in IPv4), allowing faster router processing and eliminating the checksum field. 3. **Built-in Security:** IPv6 mandates support for **IPsec**, providing authentication and confidentiality at the network layer by default. > **Examiner's Note (1 mark each):** Students who write only *"more addresses"* without the bit-lengths lose a mark. Always quantify! --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] *What is the role of the Home Agent in Mobile IPv6?* **Model Answer:** The **Home Agent (HA)** is a router on the Mobile Node's **home network** that: - Maintains a **binding cache** mapping the MN's permanent home address to its current **Care-of Address (CoA)**. - **Intercepts** packets destined to the MN's home address and **tunnels** them to the CoA when the MN is away. - Forwards **Binding Acknowledgements** to confirm registration of the new CoA. > **Pitfall:** Writing only *"it forwards packets"* is incomplete. Mention **binding cache** and **tunneling** to score full 3 marks. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (ESE 14-Mark Module Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning** > The most common mistakes in Future Internet questions are: > 1. **Conflating SDN and NFV** — they are *complementary*, not the same. > 2. **Forgetting the MIPv6 Binding Update (BU)** sequence — the protocol flow is mandatory. > 3. **Writing IPv6 advantages without technical depth** — mention 128-bit, 40-byte header, SLAAC, no broadcast. > 4. **Confusing NDN with HTTP caching** — NDN caching is *router-level and name-based*, not server-side. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — July 2024] #### (a) Explain the architecture of Mobile IPv6. Illustrate the steps involved in location update when a Mobile Node moves from one foreign network to another. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **1. Architecture (4 marks):** Mobile IPv6 consists of three key components: - **Mobile Node (MN):** A device that can change its point of attachment to the Internet while maintaining ongoing sessions. - **Home Agent (HA):** A router on the MN's home link that maintains a **Binding Cache** of (Home Address → Care-of Address) mappings. - **Correspondent Node (CN):** Any peer communicating with the MN; can be optimized via **Route Optimization**. Each MN has two addresses: - **Home Address (HoA):** Permanent, topologically belongs to the home network. - **Care-of Address (CoA):** Temporary, assigned by the visited network. **2. Handover Steps (3 marks):** | Step | Action | |---|---| | **1. Detection** | MN receives Router Advertisement from new foreign network. | | **2. CoA Acquisition** | MN forms a new CoA using IPv6 SLAAC + DAD. | | **3. Binding Update** | MN sends a BU message to HA with new CoA. | | **4. Acknowledgement** | HA sends Binding Acknowledgement (BA) and updates its cache. | | **5. Route Optimization** | MN sends BU directly to CN, enabling triangular-routing-free data flow. | > **Diagrammatic Expectation:** Examiner expects a sequence diagram similar to **Section 4.2** of these notes. --- #### (b) With a neat block diagram, describe the **Software Defined Networking (SDN)** architecture. List any four advantages of SDN. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **1. Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the **Section 4.3** SDN three-layer architecture: Application Layer → Control Layer (SDN Controller) → Infrastructure Layer (OpenFlow Switches), connected via **Northbound APIs** (top) and **Southbound APIs** (bottom, primarily **OpenFlow**). **2. Working (2 marks):** The SDN Controller installs **flow rules** into the switches' flow tables. Each packet is matched against the highest-priority rule, and the corresponding **action** (forward/drop/modify) is executed. The control plane is *logically centralized* but *physically distributed* for redundancy. **3. Four Advantages (2 marks, 0.5 each):** 1. **Programmability** — Networks can be reconfigured via software without physical changes. 2. **Centralized Management** — Single pane of glass for thousands of devices. 3. **Vendor Neutrality** — OpenFlow works across multi-vendor hardware. 4. **Dynamic Scalability** — New services (e.g., IoT QoS slices) can be spun up in seconds. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] #### (a) Discuss the features and packet types of **Named Data Networking (NDN)**. Compare NDN with the traditional IP-based Internet. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **1. NDN Features (3 marks):** - **Content-Centric** — Named data is the primitive, not hosts. - **In-network Caching** — Every router has a Content Store (CS). - **Stateful Forwarding Plane** — Routers maintain a Pending Interest Table (PIT) and Forwarding Information Base (FIB). - **Native Mobility & Multicast** — Producer can move; Data can satisfy multiple pending Interests. - **Security at Content Level** — Each Data packet is signed, not tied to a connection. **2. Packet Types (2 marks):** - **Interest Packet** — Carries the *name* of the desired content (e.g., `/kerala/news/video.mp4`); consumes one PIT entry per router. - **Data Packet** — Carries the named content + digital signature; satisfies the Interest and is cached. **3. Comparison Table (2 marks):** | Aspect | IP Internet | NDN | |---|---|---| | Primitive | Host (IP address) | Named content | | Communication | End-to-end | Pull-based, opportunistic | | Caching | Application-level (CDN) | Native in every router | | Mobility | Requires Mobile IP | Built-in | | Security | Channel-based (TLS) | Content-based (signatures) | --- #### (b) Explain **Network Function Virtualization (NFV)**. How is it different from SDN? Why is NFV critical for IoT deployments? **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **1. NFV Definition (2 marks):** NFV, standardized by **ETSI**, replaces dedicated hardware appliances (routers, firewalls, load balancers) with **software Virtual Network Functions (VNFs)** running on commodity **x86 servers** orchestrated by a **MANO** (Management and Orchestration) framework. **2. NFV Architecture (2 marks):** Three layers — **VNFs** (functional software), **NFVI** (compute/storage/network virtualization infrastructure), **MANO/OSS/BSS** (orchestration and operations support systems). **3. NFV vs SDN — Key Distinctions (2 marks):** | Aspect | NFV | SDN | |---|---|---| | **What it virtualizes** | Network *functions/services* | Network *control plane* | | **Goal** | Replace hardware boxes with software | Programmability of forwarding | | **Standards body** | ETSI | ONF | | **Typical use** | Virtualized EPC, vCPE, vFW | OpenFlow-based campus/WAN | | **Relationship** | Often *used together* with SDN | Often *used together* with NFV | **4. IoT Criticality (1 mark):** IoT deployments require **elastic, on-demand scaling** of gateways, security, and analytics. NFV enables service providers to instantiate a **vFirewall** or **vDPI** for a specific IoT tenant in minutes — impossible with rigid hardware. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — "What to write the night before the exam"** - ☐ **IPv4 = 2³²**, **IPv6 = 2¹²⁸**, IPv6 header = **40 bytes**, min MTU = **1280 bytes**. - ☐ **MIPv6 triad:** Mobile Node + Home Agent + Correspondent Node; key message = **Binding Update (BU)**. - ☐ **MIPv6 Route Optimization** avoids triangular routing. - ☐ **SDN = 3 layers** (App, Control, Infra); southbound API = **OpenFlow**; examples of controllers = **OpenDaylight, ONOS, Ryu, POX, Floodlight**. - ☐ **NFV** standardizes via **ETSI**; example VNFs = **vFirewall, vRouter, vDPI**. - ☐ **SDN ≠ NFV** — SDN = control/data plane split; NFV = function/hardware split. - ☐ **NDN = CCN**; packet types = **Interest, Data**; router structures = **CS, PIT, FIB**. - ☐ **NDN mobility is free** because no host-identity is required. - ☐ **5G peak rate = 10 Gbps** (eMBB), **latency < 1 ms** (URLLC), **mMTC = 1 million devices/km²**. - ☐ IoT drivers of Future Internet: address exhaustion, mobility, scale, security, programmability. - ☐ **SLAAC** = Stateless Address Autoconfiguration in IPv6 — no DHCP needed. - ☐ **IPsec** is mandatory in IPv6 (recommended in IPv4). - ☐ Always draw a **diagram** in 14-mark answers — it secures 2–3 easy marks.

topics.title: Review of Sensing and Actuation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT612 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Review of Sensing and Actuation # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Sensing? A **sensor** is an electronic, electromechanical, or electromechanical device specifically engineered to detect physical, chemical, or biological phenomena from the surrounding environment and convert this information into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance, or frequency). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** A sensor is a transducer that converts a physical quantity (stimulus) into an equivalent electrical signal which can be processed, displayed, and transmitted within an IoT system. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of sensors as the **five human senses** of an IoT system: - **Eyes** → Cameras, Light sensors (Photodiodes) - **Ears** → Microphones, Ultrasonic sensors - **Skin (touch)** → Pressure sensors, Temperature sensors - **Nose** → Gas sensors, Chemical sensors - **Body balance** → Accelerometers, Gyroscopes Just as our brain processes sensory input to make decisions, the IoT microcontroller processes sensor data to trigger actions. ## 1.2 What is Actuation? An **actuator** is the exact opposite of a sensor. It is a device that converts an **electrical control signal** into a **physical action** such as motion, heat, light, or force. Actuators are the "muscles" of the IoT system. > [!IMPORTANT] > In the IoT paradigm: **Sensors perceive** the world, and **actuators change** the world. Together, they form the physical interface between the cyber (digital) and physical worlds. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition If sensors are the **senses**, actuators are the **muscles and limbs**. When you touch a hot pan: 1. **Sensor** (skin) detects heat 2. **Brain** (microcontroller) decides to pull back 3. **Actuator** (muscles) moves the hand away ## 1.3 The Sensing–Actuation Loop in IoT The fundamental IoT cycle operates as: > **Physical World → Sensor → Microcontroller/Cloud → Actuator → Physical World** This closed-loop interaction enables **Smart Systems** like automatic streetlights, smart homes, industrial automation, and autonomous vehicles. ## 1.4 Key Metrics and Constants | Metric | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---| | ADC Reference Voltage ($V_{ref}$) | **3.3 V** or **5 V** | | Operating Temperature | **-40°C to +85°C** (Industrial) | | Sampling Frequency | **Hz to kHz** range | | Output Signal | Voltage (V), Current (mA), Digital (bits) | | Common IoT Supply | **3.3 V DC**, **5 V DC** | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Sensors ### A. Based on Power Requirement | Type | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Active Sensors** | Require external power (excitation voltage) to operate | Thermistor, Strain gauge, RTD | | **Passive Sensors** | Generate their own electrical signal; no external power needed | Photovoltaic cell, Piezoelectric sensor, Thermocouple | ### B. Based on Output Signal | Type | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Analog Sensors** | Output continuous voltage/current proportional to input | LM35 (Temperature), Potentiometer | | **Digital Sensors** | Output discrete digital bits (0/1) or protocol data | DHT22, DS18B20, BMP280 | ### C. Based on Detection Mechanism - **Resistive**: Resistance changes with stimulus (LDR, Thermistor) - **Capacitive**: Capacitance changes (Capacitive touch sensor) - **Inductive**: Inductance changes (Proximity sensor) - **Piezoelectric**: Voltage generated by mechanical stress - **Optical**: Light intensity changes (Photodiode, IR sensor) ## 2.2 Classification of Actuators | Actuator Type | Working Principle | IoT Application | |---|---|---| | **DC Motor** | Converts DC electrical energy to rotation | Wheels of robot, Fan | | **Servo Motor** | Precise angular position control (0°–180°) | Robotic arm, Door lock | | **Stepper Motor** | Discrete step rotation for precision | 3D printer, CNC | | **Relay** | Electromagnetic switch (ON/OFF) | Home appliance control | | **Solenoid** | Linear motion from magnetic field | Door lock, Valve control | | **Piezo Buzzer** | Converts electrical signal to sound | Alarms, Notifications | | **LED** | Electroluminescence | Indicators, Displays | ## 2.3 Sensor Characteristics (Critical for KTU) ### 2.3.1 Sensitivity Sensitivity is the ratio of change in output signal to the change in input physical quantity. $$S = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$$ where: - $S$ = Sensitivity (V/°C, mV/kPa, etc.) - $\Delta y$ = Change in output signal - $\Delta x$ = Change in input stimulus ### 2.3.2 Accuracy vs Precision - **Accuracy**: How close the measured value is to the true value. - **Precision**: How close repeated measurements are to each other (repeatability). ### 2.3.3 Linearity A sensor is **linear** if its output is directly proportional to input. Linearity error is the maximum deviation from a straight-line fit. $$\text{Linearity Error} = \frac{\Delta y_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$$ ### 2.3.4 Range and Resolution - **Range**: Min and Max measurable input values - **Resolution**: Smallest detectable change in input $$\text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{Range}}{2^n - 1}$$ where $n$ is the number of ADC bits. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---|---| | $S = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Sensitivity of sensor | V/unit | | $\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | ADC Resolution | V/bit | | $V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{in}$ | Voltage Divider (used with resistive sensors) | V | | $\text{Error} (\%) = \frac{\vert V_{measured} - V_{true} \vert}{V_{true}} \times 100$ | Percentage Error | % | | $V_{adc} = \text{Digital Value} \times \text{Resolution}$ | ADC Digital-to-Voltage Conversion | V | | $\tau = R \times C$ | RC Time Constant (response time) | seconds | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Utility:** In industrial IoT, selecting a sensor with high accuracy, appropriate range, and proper linearity ensures reliable monitoring of temperature, pressure, and flow in smart factories. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: ADC Resolution for an IoT Sensor **Problem Context:** An IoT temperature sensor (LM35) outputs **0 mV to 1000 mV** corresponding to temperatures **0°C to 150°C**. A 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V is used. Determine the resolution per °C. ### Step 1: Identify ADC Parameters The number of discrete levels for an $n$-bit ADC is: $$N = 2^n - 1$$ Substituting $n = 10$: $$N = 2^{10} - 1 = 1024 - 1 = 1023$$ ### Step 2: Calculate ADC Voltage Resolution The voltage step per LSB (Least Significant Bit) is: $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{3.3}{1023}$$ $$V_{LSB} \approx 0.003226 \text{ V} = 3.226 \text{ mV/bit}$$ ### Step 3: Calculate Sensor Sensitivity The LM35 output is **10 mV per °C**: $$S_{sensor} = 10 \text{ mV/°C}$$ ### Step 4: Compute the Combined Resolution The temperature change required to change 1 LSB of ADC is: $$\Delta T_{resolution} = \frac{V_{LSB}}{S_{sensor}}$$ $$\Delta T_{resolution} = \frac{3.226 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}}$$ $$\Delta T_{resolution} \approx 0.3226 \text{ °C/LSB}$$ > [!NOTE] > This means the smallest temperature change the IoT system can detect is approximately **0.32 °C**. This is critical when designing medical or precision agriculture IoT applications. ## 3.2 Derivation: Sensor Linearity Error A pressure sensor is rated **0–100 kPa** producing **0–5 V** output. The actual output at **50 kPa** is measured as **2.65 V** (ideal should be 2.5 V). ### Step 1: Find the Ideal Output (Straight Line Assumption) $$V_{ideal} = \frac{50}{100} \times 5 = 2.5 \text{ V}$$ ### Step 2: Find the Deviation $$\Delta y = V_{actual} - V_{ideal} = 2.65 - 2.5 = 0.15 \text{ V}$$ ### Step 3: Calculate Linearity Error $$\text{Linearity Error} = \frac{\Delta y}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$$ $$\text{Linearity Error} = \frac{0.15}{5.0} \times 100\% = 3\%$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | LM35 (Temp) | DHT22 (Temp+Humidity) | HC-SR04 (Ultrasonic) | LDR (Light) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Type** | Analog | Digital | Digital | Analog (Resistive) | | **Range** | -55 to +150°C | -40 to 80°C, 0–100% RH | 2 cm to 400 cm | Variable resistance | | **Interface** | ADC Pin | Single-wire protocol | Trigger/Echo pins | Voltage Divider | | **Accuracy** | ±0.5°C | ±0.5°C, ±2% RH | ±3 mm | Non-linear | | **Power** | 3.3–5V, 60 µA | 3–5V, 1.5 mA | 5V, 15 mA | Passive | | **IoT Use** | Weather station | Smart agriculture | Distance/level sensing | Automatic streetlight | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete IoT Sensing-Actuation Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical World] --> B[Sensor] B --> C[Signal Conditioning] C --> D[ADC Conversion] D --> E[Microcontroller / Cloud] E --> F[DAC / PWM Generator] F --> G[Driver Circuit] G --> H[Actuator] H --> I[Physical Action on World] I --> A classDef sensorStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef processStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef actuatorStyle fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef worldStyle fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,I worldStyle class B sensorStyle class C,D,E,F,G processStyle class H actuatorStyle ``` ## 4.2 Sensor Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB S[Sensors] --> A1[Active Sensors] S --> P1[Passive Sensors] S --> AN1[Analog Sensors] S --> DG1[Digital Sensors] A1 --> A1a[Thermistor] A1 --> A1b[Strain Gauge] A1 --> A1c[RTD] P1 --> P1a[Photovoltaic Cell] P1 --> P1b[Piezoelectric] P1 --> P1c[Thermocouple] AN1 --> AN1a[LM35 Temperature] AN1 --> AN1b[Potentiometer] AN1 --> AN1c[LDR] DG1 --> DG1a[DHT22] DG1 --> DG1b[DS18B20] DG1 --> DG1c[BMP280] classDef rootStyle fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef activeStyle fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef passiveStyle fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef analogStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef digitalStyle fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class S rootStyle class A1,A1a,A1b,A1c activeStyle class P1,P1a,P1b,P1c passiveStyle class AN1,AN1a,AN1b,AN1c analogStyle class DG1,DG1a,DG1b,DG1c digitalStyle ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Smart Room IoT System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph InputSensors [SENSING LAYER] TS[Temperature Sensor LM35] MS[Motion Sensor PIR] LS[Light Sensor LDR] GS[Gas Sensor MQ2] end subgraph ProcessingLayer [PROCESSING LAYER] MC[Microcontroller Arduino/ESP32] ADC[10-bit ADC] CLOUD[Cloud Server] end subgraph OutputActuators [ACTUATION LAYER] FAN[DC Fan Cooling] BULB[Smart LED Bulb] ALARM[Piezo Buzzer Alarm] LOCK[Servo Door Lock] end TS --> ADC LS --> ADC MS --> MC GS --> MC ADC --> MC MC --> CLOUD CLOUD --> MC MC --> FAN MC --> BULB MC --> ALARM MC --> LOCK classDef sensorGroup fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef processGroup fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef actuatorGroup fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class TS,MS,LS,GS sensorGroup class MC,ADC,CLOUD processGroup class FAN,BULB,ALARM,LOCK actuatorGroup ``` ## 4.4 Signal Conditioning Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Sensor Signal
mV range, noisy] --> B[Amplifier
Gain G] B --> C[Filter
LPF / HPF] C --> D[ADC
Analog to Digital] D --> E[Digital Output
to Microcontroller] classDef sigStyle fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef procStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,E sigStyle class B,C,D procStyle ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Differentiate between active and passive sensors with two examples each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Requires external excitation power to operate | Generates its own electrical signal | | **Energy Source** | External (voltage/current source) | Input stimulus itself | | **Examples** | Thermistor, Strain Gauge, RTD | Photovoltaic cell, Piezoelectric, Thermocouple | | **Signal Output** | Resistance/Capacitance change | Direct voltage generation | | **IoT Use** | Wheatstone bridge circuits | Solar-powered IoT nodes | > [!NOTE] > A **Thermocouple** (passive) and a **RTD** (active) both measure temperature, but RTD needs excitation current while thermocouple generates mV directly from the Seebeck effect. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **What is an actuator? List four types of actuators used in IoT systems.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** An actuator is a device that converts an electrical control signal into a physical action (motion, heat, light, or force). It is the output interface of an IoT system that performs actions in the physical world. **Four Types of Actuators:** 1. **DC Motor** – Provides continuous rotation; used in robotic wheels and fans. 2. **Servo Motor** – Provides precise angular position (0°–180°); used in robotic arms and door locks. 3. **Relay** – An electromagnetic switch; used to control high-voltage appliances from low-voltage IoT devices. 4. **Piezo Buzzer** – Converts electrical signal to sound; used in alarm and notification systems. --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the basic structure of a sensor and discuss the role of signal conditioning in IoT systems. **(7 Marks)** **b)** A thermistor has a resistance of **10 kΩ at 25°C** and **8.5 kΩ at 50°C**. Calculate the sensitivity in Ω/°C. If this thermistor is connected in a voltage divider with a **10 kΩ reference resistor** and supplied with **5 V**, find the output voltage at **50°C**. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Structure of a Sensor:** A typical sensor system consists of the following blocks: 1. **Sensing Element**: Physically interacts with the stimulus (e.g., thermistor bead, photodiode junction). 2. **Transduction Element**: Converts the physical change into an electrical signal (resistance, voltage, current). 3. **Signal Conditioning Circuit**: Amplifies, filters, and linearizes the raw signal. 4. **Output Interface**: Provides a standardized signal (0–5 V, 4–20 mA, or digital protocol) to the microcontroller. **Role of Signal Conditioning:** - **Amplification**: Boosts weak sensor signals (mV) to usable levels (V). - **Filtering**: Removes high-frequency noise and 50 Hz power line interference. - **Linearization**: Corrects non-linear sensor response. - **Isolation**: Protects the microcontroller from voltage spikes. - **ADC Matching**: Prepares the signal range to match the ADC reference voltage. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate Sensitivity** $$S = \frac{\Delta R}{\Delta T} = \frac{R_2 - R_1}{T_2 - T_1}$$ $$S = \frac{8.5 \text{ k}\Omega - 10 \text{ k}\Omega}{50°C - 25°C} = \frac{-1.5 \text{ k}\Omega}{25°C}$$ $$S = -60 \text{ Ω/°C}$$ (Negative sign indicates NTC — Negative Temperature Coefficient behavior) **Step 2: Voltage Divider Output at 50°C** $$V_{out} = \frac{R_{thermistor}}{R_{thermistor} + R_{ref}} \times V_{in}$$ $$V_{out} = \frac{8.5}{8.5 + 10} \times 5 = \frac{8.5}{18.5} \times 5$$ $$V_{out} = 0.4595 \times 5 = 2.297 \text{ V}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert kΩ to Ω consistently. Always express the final voltage with **3 decimal places** to gain full marks. Do not mix up units in voltage divider calculations. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **a)** Compare the working principles of **DC Motor**, **Servo Motor**, and **Stepper Motor** as actuators in IoT applications. Mention one IoT use case for each. **(7 Marks)** **b)** An LDR-based light sensor is used in a smart streetlight system. The LDR resistance is **5 kΩ in bright light** and **100 kΩ in darkness**. It is connected in a voltage divider with a **10 kΩ resistor** and **3.3 V supply**. The microcontroller reads the output through a **10-bit ADC**. Determine the digital ADC value when the LDR is in darkness. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Motor Comparison:** | Parameter | DC Motor | Servo Motor | Stepper Motor | |---|---|---|---| | **Principle** | Lorentz force on current-carrying conductor in magnetic field | DC motor + position feedback (potentiometer) + closed-loop control | Electromagnetic coils energize in sequence for discrete steps | | **Rotation** | Continuous (0–360°) | Limited (0–180° typical) | Discrete steps (1.8° per step typical) | | **Position Control** | Not precise (no feedback) | Highly precise via PWM feedback | Precise (open-loop) | | **PWM Required** | Speed control only | Position via PWM (1–2 ms pulse) | Coil activation sequence | | **IoT Use Case** | Smart fan speed control | Smart door lock / Robotic arm gripper | 3D printer / Smart valve | | **Cost** | Low | Medium | Medium-High | **Part (b) — ADC Calculation in Darkness:** **Step 1: Calculate Output Voltage** $$V_{out} = \frac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + R_{ref}} \times V_{in}$$ In darkness, $R_{LDR} = 100$ kΩ: $$V_{out} = \frac{100}{100 + 10} \times 3.3 = \frac{100}{110} \times 3.3$$ $$V_{out} = 0.9091 \times 3.3 = 3.000 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2: Calculate ADC Resolution** $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{1023} = 0.003226 \text{ V/bit}$$ **Step 3: Calculate Digital ADC Value** $$D = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{LSB}} = \frac{3.000}{0.003226}$$ $$D \approx 930 \text{ (in decimal)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly confuse the LDR position in the voltage divider. The output voltage formula is $\frac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + R_{ref}}$ ONLY if LDR is connected to ground. Always **draw the circuit** before applying the formula. Also, the ADC value is in **decimal**, not binary, for the final answer. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:** - **Sensor Definition**: Device that converts physical stimulus → electrical signal. - **Actuator Definition**: Device that converts electrical signal → physical action. - **Active vs Passive Sensor**: Active needs external power; Passive is self-generating. - **Analog vs Digital Sensor**: Analog = continuous voltage output; Digital = discrete bits/protocol. - **Key Sensor Characteristics**: Sensitivity, Accuracy, Precision, Linearity, Range, Resolution. - **Sensitivity Formula**: $S = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ (output change per input change). - **ADC Resolution Formula**: $V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ — **memorize this** for numerical problems. - **Voltage Divider Formula**: $V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \times V_{in}$ — used with **LDR, Thermistor, Potentiometer**. - **Common IoT Sensors**: LM35 (Temp), DHT22 (Humidity), HC-SR04 (Distance), PIR (Motion), MQ2 (Gas), LDR (Light). - **Common IoT Actuators**: DC Motor, Servo Motor, Stepper Motor, Relay, Solenoid, LED, Piezo Buzzer. - **Signal Conditioning Steps**: Amplify → Filter → Linearize → Convert. - **Servo Motor Control**: Uses **PWM (1–2 ms pulse)** for position control; rotates **0°–180°**. - **Thermistor Type**: NTC (resistance decreases with temperature) — most common in IoT. - **LDR Behavior**: Resistance is **low in light, high in darkness**. - **Standard KTU Answer Format**: Definition → Classification → Diagram → Formula → Numerical Solution → Real-world Application. - **Don't forget units** in the final answer (V, mV, °C, kΩ, bits, etc.). - **Always draw block diagrams** for sensor/actuator system questions — earns 2–3 easy marks. > **End of Module 1 Notes — Review of Sensing and Actuation**

topics.title: Basics of IoT Networking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT612 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of IoT Networking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Basics of IoT Networking — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT Networking (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition)**
> *IoT Networking* refers to the **interconnection infrastructure, protocol stack, communication models, and addressing mechanisms** that enable heterogeneous physical objects ("Things") to identify, sense, actuate, process, and exchange data over constrained networks — typically spanning **Personal Area Networks (PAN)**, **Local Area Networks (LAN)**, **Metropolitan Area Networks (MAN)**, and **Wide Area Networks (WAN)** — using both IPv4 and **IPv6** addressing over low-power wireless mediums.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the term strictly covers:
- The **layered protocol architecture** (Perception → Network → Transport → Application).
- The **four canonical IoT communication models** defined by the IoT World Forum.
- **Network connectivity technologies** classified by coverage radius, data rate, and power budget.
- **Addressing and identification** schemes adapted to constrained devices.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The Human Nervous System**
> Think of an IoT network exactly like the **human nervous system**:
> - **Sensors (Skin/Organs)** = Perception layer devices (temperature, motion, gas).
> - **Spinal Cord & Local Nerves** = PAN/LAN (e.g., ZigBee inside a smart home).
> - **Brain & Memory** = Cloud / Fog servers (Processing layer).
> - **Internet (Telephone Wires of the Body)** = WAN backhaul (4G/5G/Fiber).
> - **Action — "Move your hand!"** = Application layer (actuator commands flowing back).

Just as a reflex arc skips the brain for speed (latency-critical control), an IoT gateway can locally process data without going to the cloud. **Networking in IoT is essentially the wiring of this "digital nervous system."**

## 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

The examiner will expect these benchmark figures to be recalled verbatim:

- **IEEE 802.15.4** (the radio foundation of ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, Thread) — operates in the **2.4 GHz** ISM band with **250 kbps** raw data rate, **868 MHz** in Europe at **20 kbps**, and **915 MHz** in USA at **40 kbps**.
- **IPv6 address space** = **2¹²⁸** addresses (≈ **3.4 × 10³⁸** unique nodes).
- **Maximum IPv6 header size** = **40 bytes** (fixed, no fragmentation in source).
- **6LoWPAN** = IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks — supports a Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU) as low as **127 bytes** vs. Ethernet's **1500 bytes**.
- **Sigfox** range ≈ **30–50 km** in rural, **3–10 km** urban.
- **LoRaWAN** range ≈ **2–5 km** urban, **15 km** rural.

## 1.4 Why "Networking" is Hard in IoT (The Big Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Fundamental Constraints that Differentiate IoT Networking from Traditional IP Networking:**
> 1. **Resource Constraints** — limited CPU (8–32 bit), RAM (tens of KB), and energy (battery-powered years).
> 2. **Heterogeneity** — devices speak Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, LoRa, Modbus, CAN, etc.
> 3. **Scale** — billions of nodes demand **stateless address auto-configuration (SLAAC)** rather than DHCP.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IoT Network Architecture — Layered View

The **IoT World Forum Reference Model** (Cisco-anchored) and the **modified OSI 5-layer model** are both examinable. KTU predominantly uses the simplified **4-layer model**:

| Layer | KTU Term | Function | Example Protocols / Devices |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. **Perception / Sensing** | Sensor Layer | Converts physical phenomena → electrical signals | DHT22, MQ-135, Accelerometer, RFID tag |
| 2. **Network / Transport** | Communication Layer | Routes data between device and server | Wi-Fi, 6LoWPAN, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, RPL |
| 3. **Processing / Middleware** | Edge/Fog/Cloud | Stores, analyzes, and decides | MQTT broker, AWS IoT Core, Node-RED |
| 4. **Application** | Service Layer | Delivers user-facing functionality | Smart home app, industrial dashboard |

## 2.2 The Four IoT Communication Models (HIGH-YIELD — KTU 2024)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must Memorize — Examiner Favourite:**
> 1. **Device-to-Device (D2D)** — direct M2M, no intermediary (e.g., BLE phone ↔ smartwatch).
> 2. **Device-to-Cloud (D2C)** — sensor → cloud directly over IP (e.g., Wi-Fi ESP32 → AWS).
> 3. **Device-to-Gateway (D2G)** — sensor → local gateway → cloud (e.g., ZigBee hub → cloud).
> 4. **Back-End Data-Sharing** — multiple applications share a single device's data stream via a service bus.

A fifth modern extension recognized in KTU 2024 is **Device-to-Application-Layer-Translation (DALT)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C$ | Channel Capacity (Shannon) | $C = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bits/second (bps) |
| $B$ | Bandwidth | given | Hz |
| $S/N$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | linear scale (NOT dB) | dimensionless |
| $N$ | Total IPv6 addresses | $2^{128}$ | count |
| $R_{raw}$ | IEEE 802.15.4 raw rate (2.4 GHz) | **250 000** | bps |
| $T_{frame}$ | Air-time of an 802.15.4 frame | $T_{frame} = \dfrac{L_{header} + L_{payload}}{R_{raw}}$ | seconds |
| $D_{req}$ | Total devices addressable | $\sum_{i=0}^{7} 16 \cdot 16^{i} = 2^{128}$ | nodes |
| $L_{6LoWPAN}$ | 6LoWPAN compressed header | as low as **2 bytes** (RFC 6282) | bytes |
| $MTU$ | Max payload over 802.15.4 | **81** (after 25-byte MAC overhead) | bytes |
| $\eta$ | Spectral efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{C}{B}$ | bits/s/Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Note for KTU Exam Hall:** When a problem gives SNR in **dB**, you MUST convert using $S/N = 10^{\text{SNR}_{dB}/10}$ before plugging into Shannon's equation. Failing this conversion is the **#1 mark-deduction reason** in KTU Board evaluations.

## 2.4 Network Topology Choices in IoT

| Topology | Pros | Cons | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Star** | Simple, low power at nodes | Single point of failure | Smart home, wearables |
| **Mesh** | Self-healing, high range | Routing complexity, energy | Industrial IoT (ZigBee/Thread) |
| **Tree / Cluster-Tree** | Hierarchical, scalable | Root failure cascades | Smart metering, agriculture |
| **Hybrid (Star-of-Mesh)** | Balanced | Costly design | Smart city deployments |

## 2.5 Protocol Stack — Mapping IoT Protocols to OSI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU often asks to "map MQTT/CoAP to the OSI stack." The standard answer:
> - MQTT/CoAP = **Application Layer** (Layer 7).
> - UDP = **Transport Layer** (Layer 4) for CoAP; TCP for MQTT.
> - 6LoWPAN = **Network + Adaptation** (Layer 3 + a shim).
> - IEEE 802.15.4 = **MAC + PHY** (Layers 1 & 2).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: A refinery deploys **6LoWPAN mesh** over 802.15.4 across pressure valves, with a **D2G gateway** forwarding Modbus-over-TCP to a SCADA cloud. Networking layer ensures deterministic sub-100 ms latency.
- **Precision Agriculture**: **LoRaWAN** D2C connectivity across hectares saves fiber trenching; SF (Spreading Factor) trade-off is a *networking* decision.
- **Smart Healthcare**: BLE D2D for wearables (low energy), Wi-Fi D2C for ICU monitors (high bandwidth), 5G D2C for ambulance telemetry (low latency).

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive, no-step-skipped derivations aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme expected working.**

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Number of IoT Devices an IPv6 Subnet Can Carry

An IPv6 address is **128 bits**. The standard /64 prefix is reserved per link in 6LoWPAN networks.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total IPv6 addresses} &= 2^{128} \\
&= 2^{64} \times 2^{64} \quad \text{(split into 64-bit network prefix + 64-bit IID)} \\
\text{Devices per /64 subnet} &= 2^{64} \\
2^{64} &= 2^{10} \times 2^{10} \times 2^{10} \times 2^{10} \times 2^{10} \times 2^{4} \\
&= 1024 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 16 \\
&\approx 1.8446744 \times 10^{19} \text{ devices per subnet.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation of the chain:**
1. The IPv6 header reserves a **64-bit Interface ID (IID)** for each device.
2. The remaining **64 bits** form the network prefix assigned by the router (via SLAAC).
3. Each /64 prefix therefore supports $2^{64}$ unique hosts.
4. $2^{64} = 1.8446744 \times 10^{19}$ devices — roughly the number of grains of sand on 50 Earths.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Air-Time of a 6LoWPAN / 802.15.4 Frame (Vital KTU Numerical)

A temperature sensor sends a 20-byte payload using IEEE 802.15.4 at 2.4 GHz (250 kbps).

**Step 1 — Compute total frame size.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= L_{MAC\,header} + L_{payload} + L_{FCS} \\
L_{MAC\,header} &= 25 \text{ bytes (max)} \\
L_{payload} &= 20 \text{ bytes} \\
L_{FCS} &= 2 \text{ bytes} \\
L_{total} &= 25 + 20 + 2 = 47 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert bytes to bits.**

$$
L_{bits} = 47 \times 8 = 376 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute air-time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= \dfrac{L_{bits}}{R_{raw}} \\
&= \dfrac{376}{250\,000} \\
&= 1.504 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\
&= 1.504 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — If 6LoWPAN compression (RFC 6282) is used, the IP+UDP header shrinks from 28+8 = 36 bytes to as low as 2 + 4 = 6 bytes. Recalculate payload-side savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{saved} &= (36 - 6) \text{ bytes} = 30 \text{ bytes} \\
L_{compressed\,total} &= 25 + 20 + 2 = 47 \text{ bytes (MAC untouched, compression inside payload)} \\
T_{compressed} &= \dfrac{47 \times 8}{250\,000} = 1.504 \text{ ms (same, as 802.15.4 still carries the bytes)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual takeaway:** 6LoWPAN saves *energy per node* (fewer bytes to transmit on air reduces duty cycle proportionally) but air-time on the channel is governed by the underlying 802.15.4 PHY rate, which is **fixed at 250 kbps** in the 2.4 GHz band.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shannon Channel Capacity Applied to an IoT Link

A 6LoWPAN channel in the 2.4 GHz band has $B = 2$ MHz and SNR = 20 dB. Find max error-free bit rate.

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_{linear} &= 10^{\text{SNR}_{dB}/10} \\
&= 10^{20/10} = 10^{2} = 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right) \\
&= 2 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_{2}(1 + 100) \\
&= 2 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_{2}(101)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $\log_{2}(101)$.**

$$
\log_{2}(101) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(101)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \dfrac{2.0043}{0.3010} \approx 6.6582
$$

**Step 4 — Final numerical answer.**

$$
C = 2 \times 10^{6} \times 6.6582 \approx 13.316 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even though 802.15.4 caps the practical rate at 0.25 Mbps, the channel *theoretically* could carry ~53× more; the gap represents the **link-budget safety margin** that makes IoT robust against interference and fading.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Header Overhead Ratio of IPv6 vs 6LoWPAN

Original IPv6+UDP packet = $40 + 8 = 48$ bytes. After 6LoWPAN IPHC compression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compressed header} &= 2 \text{ bytes (IPHC) } + 4 \text{ bytes (UDP_NHC) } \\
&= 6 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Overhead ratio} &= \dfrac{6}{48} = 0.125 = 12.5\% \\
\text{Saving} &= 87.5\% \text{ of header bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 20-byte sensor payload, total packet drops from 68 → 26 bytes, **a 61.7% total reduction** — directly translating to **61.7% lower radio-on time** and thus battery savings.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — IoT Wireless Technologies

| Parameter | Wi-Fi (802.11n) | Bluetooth LE | ZigBee (802.15.4) | LoRaWAN | NB-IoT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz / 868 / 915 MHz | 433/868/915 MHz | Licensed LTE bands |
| Range | ~50 m | ~10–100 m | ~10–100 m | 2–15 km | ~1–10 km |
| Data Rate | 150 Mbps | 2 Mbps | 250 kbps | 0.3–50 kbps | 26 kbps DL / 62 kbps UL |
| Power | High | Low | Low | Very low | Medium |
| Topology | Star | Star / Mesh | Mesh | Star-of-stars | Star (cellular) |
| IPv6 native? | Yes | No (v6 over BLE: experimental) | Via 6LoWPAN | No (proprietary) | Yes (3GPP) |
| Typical Use | Video, browsing | Wearables, audio | Home automation, sensor mesh | Smart agriculture, metering | Smart city, utilities |
