**Level 5 — Legal / Governance Interoperability:**
This layer addresses *data ownership, privacy, sovereignty, and regulatory compliance*. Standards: **GDPR, India's DPDP Act 2023, HIPAA, ISO 27001, NIST CSF**. *Example:* A European patient's wearable data cannot leave EU servers without explicit consent.

#### (b) Discuss the major IoT interoperability standards and reference architectures. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The IoT ecosystem has produced several **reference architectures** to unify interoperability:

**1. oneM2M:**
A horizontal service-platform standard that sits above device protocols. It provides a **Common Services Entity (CSE)** with functions like registration, data management, subscription, security, and device management. *Interoperability role:* Abstracts device protocols (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP) into a unified RESTful resource tree.

**2. OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA):**
An industrial standard providing **secure, reliable, platform-independent** communication. It includes a rich **information modelling** layer (using nodes, references, and types). *Interoperability role:* Maps Modbus, Profinet, EtherCAT into a unified namespace; widely adopted in **Industry 4.0**.

**3. W3C Web of Things (WoT):**
Defines a **Thing Description (TD)** in JSON-LD that describes the capabilities, data schemas, and interaction patterns of any IoT device. *Interoperability role:* Uses W3C standards (JSON-LD, RDF) to give a common *semantic description* of devices across vendors.

**4. FIWARE:**
A European open-source IoT platform providing **NGSI** context-broker APIs for unified data access.

**5. IETF Protocols — MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, AMQP:**
These are the *transport-level* standards. MQTT is publish/subscribe, CoAP is request/response over UDP, AMQP is queue-based.

**6. OCF (Open Connectivity Foundation):**
Provides a unified API for consumer IoT devices (smart-home).

**Comparison Table:**

| Standard | Layer | Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **MQTT** | Transport | Lightweight pub/sub for constrained devices |
| **CoAP** | Transport | RESTful over UDP, low overhead |
| **OPC UA** | Syntactic + Semantic | Industrial-grade with info models |
| **oneM2M** | Cross-layer (platform) | Service-layer abstraction |
| **WoT (W3C)** | Semantic | Thing Descriptions in JSON-LD |
| **FIWARE NGSI** | Application | Context-broker APIs |

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Explain the role of semantic web technologies (RDF, OWL, SPARQL) in achieving IoT interoperability. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

Semantic web technologies provide a **formal, machine-readable framework** for representing the *meaning* of IoT data, enabling Semantic Interoperability (LCIM Level 3+).

**1. RDF (Resource Description Framework):**
RDF is the **foundational data model** of the semantic web. It represents knowledge as **triples**:
$$ \text{Subject} \xrightarrow{\text{Predicate}} \text{Object} $$
*Example:*
```turtle
<sensor_42> <measures> <body_temperature> .
<body_temperature> <unit> "Fahrenheit" .
```
RDF is **vendor-neutral** and can be serialized as **RDF/XML, JSON-LD, N-Triples, Turtle**. This solves the syntactic heterogeneity problem.

**2. RDFS (RDF Schema):**
Extends RDF with **class hierarchies and property definitions** — e.g., `TemperatureSensor rdfs:subClassOf Sensor`.

**3. OWL (Web Ontology Language):**
Adds **rich logical constructs** — equivalence, transitivity, cardinality restrictions, disjointness. OWL allows reasoning engines (e.g., **Apache Jena, Pellet, Hermit**) to *infer* new facts from existing ones.
*Example:* If `BodyThermometer measures BodyTemperature` and `Fever is BodyTemperature > 38°C`, the system can **automatically detect fever**.

**4. SPARQL:**
The **query language** for RDF graphs. It is to RDF what SQL is to relational databases.
*Example query:*
```sparql
SELECT ?sensor ?temp WHERE {
  ?sensor saref:measures saref:Temperature .
  ?sensor saref:hasValue ?temp .
  FILTER(?temp > 38)
}
```

**Domain-Specific Ontologies Used in IoT:**

| Ontology | Domain |
|---|---|
| **SSN / SOSA** | W3C standard for sensors and observations |
| **SAREF** | Smart Appliances REFerence ontology (ETSI) |
| **IoT-Lite** | Lightweight ontology for resource-constrained devices |
| **Smart Appliances Ontology (SAO)** | Energy management |
| **FIESTA-IoT** | Federated Interoperability |

**Outcome:** Semantic web technologies transform IoT from a *"sea of bytes"* into a *"sea of meaning"*, enabling cross-vendor, cross-domain reasoning.

#### (b) Describe the oneM2M architecture. How does it address IoT interoperability? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**oneM2M** is a global standard developed by **ETSI, TIA, ATIS, CCSA, TTA, ARIB, TSDSI** (representing 7 SDOs from 7 regions) to provide a **horizontal service layer** for IoT.

**Architecture — Three-Layer Model:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app["APPLICATION LAYER\nAE Application Entity"]:::app
    serv["COMMON SERVICES LAYER\nCSE Common Services Entity"]:::serv
    net["NETWORK SERVICES LAYER\nNSE Underlying network"]:::net

    app --> serv
    serv --> net

    classDef app fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef serv fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#fde68a,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000
```

**Node Types:**

- **Application Service Node (ASN)** — hosts AE only.
- **Common Services Node (CSN)** — hosts CSE only.
- **Application Dedicated Node (ADN)** — hosts both AE and CSE on a constrained device.
- **Middle Node (MN)** — host CSE for inter-CSE communication.
- **Infrastructure Node (IN)** — central CSE of a service provider.

**Common Services Functions (CSFs):**
- **Registration** — devices announce themselves.
- **Data Management & Repository** — stores resources in a tree structure.
- **Device Management** — remote configuration, firmware updates.
- **Subscription & Notification** — pub/sub.
- **Security** — authentication, authorization, encryption (TLS, DTLS, OAuth).
- **Communication Management** — handles MQTT, CoAP, HTTP uniformly via **Mca/Mcc/Mca'** reference points.

**How oneM2M Achieves Interoperability:**

1. **Protocol Abstraction** — Apps use a **uniform RESTful API** (Mca) regardless of underlying transport.
2. **Semantic Resource Tree** — All data, devices, and apps are exposed as **resources** in a tree (similar to a file system), with semantic descriptions.
3. **Cross-Domain** — Works equally for smart cities, healthcare, industrial, automotive.
4. **Vendor Neutrality** — Multilateral SDO governance prevents vendor lock-in.
5. **Horizontal Reusability** — A single oneM2M platform can serve multiple verticals.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students frequently confuse **oneM2M with OPC UA**. Remember:
> - **oneM2M** = Horizontal **service-layer** platform (ETSI-led, application-agnostic).
> - **OPC UA** = Vertical **communication + information model** (industrial, strong in PLCs).
> Mark deduction of **2 marks** is common if you interchange them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Interoperability in IoT)**

- **Definition:** Ability of heterogeneous IoT systems to exchange, interpret, and use data seamlessly.
- **EIF Layers (4 + 1):** Technical → Syntactic → Semantic → Organizational → (Legal).
- **LCIM Levels (0–6):** No → Technical → Syntactic → Semantic → Pragmatic → Dynamic → Conceptual.
- **Syntactic Interoperability** = Common *format* (JSON, XML, Protobuf).
- **Semantic Interoperability** = Common *meaning* (RDF, OWL, SPARQL, ontologies).
- **Key Semantic Tech:** RDF triples, OWL reasoning, SPARQL queries, JSON-LD.
- **Key IoT Ontologies:** SSN/SOSA, SAREF, IoT-Lite, FIESTA-IoT.
- **Key Transport Protocols:** MQTT (pub/sub, TCP), CoAP (RESTful, UDP), AMQP (queue), HTTP.
- **Key Service Architectures:** oneM2M (horizontal), OPC UA (industrial), W3C WoT (semantic), FIWARE (context broker).
- **Formulas to memorize:**
  - $IML = \frac{\sum w_i L_i}{W_{total}}$
  - $FI = 1 - \frac{N_{shared}}{N_{total}}$
  - $DLT = \frac{\vert D_{source} - D_{mapped} \vert}{D_{source}} \times 100\%$
  - $CDL = T_{parse} + T_{map} + T_{serialize} + T_{transmit}$
- **Challenges:** Heterogeneity, lack of common semantics, scalability, security, privacy, governance.
- **Trends to mention:** Digital Twins, WoT Thing Descriptions, NGSI-LD (Linked Data), Self-Describing Devices.
- **Examiner's golden rule:** Always state the **layer (EIF)** and **level (LCIM)** when describing any interoperability solution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 30-Second Memory Hook for the Exam Hall:**
> *"Transport talks, Syntactic agrees, Semantic understands, Organisational collaborates, Legal permits."*
> Memorize this single sentence to recall all 5 EIF layers in correct ascending order.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Arduino Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT612 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Arduino Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Arduino Programming** is the process of writing, compiling, and uploading **sketches** (source-code files written in a simplified dialect of C/C++) to an Arduino microcontroller board using the **Arduino Integrated Development Environment (IDE)**. The language is officially termed the **Arduino Programming Language**, which leverages the **AVR Libc** (or equivalent HAL) library functions to abstract low-level register manipulation into intuitive, human-readable function calls.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECPT612 – IoT and Applications)**, Arduino programming constitutes the **practical application layer** of the IoT stack. It is the bridge between the **physical perception layer** (sensors) and the **network/application layer** (cloud/dashboards). The reference board for academic study is the **Arduino UNO R3**, which is built around the **ATmega328P** 8-bit AVR RISC microcontroller operating at a clock frequency of **16 MHz**, with **32 KB** of flash program memory, **2 KB** of SRAM, and **1 KB** of EEPROM.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Universal Remote Control"**
> Imagine you have just received a sophisticated electronic device (a fan, a curtain, a lamp), but it speaks only in a foreign language. You cannot directly tell it what to do. The **Arduino** acts as your **personal translator**: you speak simple English commands in the IDE (your "telephone"), the IDE converts them into machine code (translation), and uploads it via the **USB cable** (the courier) to the Arduino board. The board then executes these commands **forever in a loop**, translating sensor inputs (light, temperature, motion) into actuator outputs (LEDs, motors, buzzers). Programming the Arduino is essentially *teaching this translator new sentences*.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Sketch** | A source-code file (`.ino`) containing the Arduino program |
| **IDE** | Integrated Development Environment — the software used to write, verify, and upload code |
| **MCU** | Microcontroller Unit — the brain (e.g., ATmega328P) |
| **GPIO** | General Purpose Input/Output — the physical pins |
| **Bootloader** | A small pre-loaded program that allows code upload via USB/UART without an external programmer |
| **Bare-Metal** | Programming without an operating system — direct control of the hardware |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The course explicitly mandates familiarity with the **Arduino IDE interface**, **sketch anatomy**, **built-in functions** (digital/analog I/O, timing, serial), **data types**, and **control structures**, all of which are covered exhaustively in the sections that follow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Arduino UNO R3 — Hardware Reference

Before writing code, you must understand the **execution environment**. The Arduino UNO exposes **14 digital I/O pins** (of which **6** support PWM output, marked with `~`) and **6 analog input pins** (A0–A5). The operating voltage is **5 V DC**, with a recommended input range of **7–12 V**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin Categories at a Glance:**
> - **Digital Pins 0–13:** Used for `HIGH` (5 V) or `LOW` (0 V) discrete signalling.
> - **PWM Pins 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11:** Emulate analog output using ~**490 Hz** (or ~**980 Hz** on pins 5 & 6) fast-switching pulses.
> - **Analog Pins A0–A5:** Read continuous voltages in the range **0 V – 5 V**, mapping to integer values **0 – 1023** (10-bit ADC).
> - **Pins 0 (RX) & 1 (TX):** Reserved for hardware serial communication; avoid using them as GPIO unless disconnected from USB.

## 2.2 The Arduino Software Stack

The Arduino programming model consists of three concentric layers:

1. **Sketch Layer (User Code):** The `.ino` file written by the developer — simple, high-level, and readable.
2. **Core/Library Layer:** The `Arduino.h` core and contributed libraries (e.g., `Servo.h`, `Wire.h`, `SPI.h`) that wrap hardware registers into callable functions.
3. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):** Direct register-level code generated by the IDE's toolchain (AVR-GCC for UNO).

## 2.3 KTU Formula & Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every essential equation, conversion, and timing constant you must memorise for the examination.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| **ADC Conversion (Analog → Digital)** | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 1024 \right\rfloor$ | $D$ = digital output (0–1023), $V_{in}$ = input voltage (V), $V_{ref}$ = reference voltage (5 V default) |
| **PWM Duty Cycle → Output Voltage (approx.)** | $V_{out} = \dfrac{D}{255} \times 5\,\text{V}$ | $D$ = duty value (0–255) |
| **Voltage → ADC Reverse** | $V_{in} = \dfrac{D}{1023} \times V_{ref}$ | — |
| **Resolution of ADC** | $2^{n} = 1024 \rightarrow n = 10\,\text{bits}$ | $n$ = bit depth |
| **Resolution of PWM** | $2^{m} = 256 \rightarrow m = 8\,\text{bits}$ | $m$ = bit depth |
| **Sampling Rate (Prescaler 128 default)** | $f_{ADC} \approx 9.6\,\text{kHz}$ | — |
| **Clock Period** | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}} = \dfrac{1}{16\,\text{MHz}} = 62.5\,\text{ns}$ | $f_{clk}$ = crystal frequency |
| **Instruction Cycle** | $T_{inst} = \dfrac{T_{clk}}{1} = 62.5\,\text{ns}$ (1 cycle/instruction) | — |
| **Map Function (linear scaling)** | $y = \dfrac{(x - in_{min})(out_{max} - out_{min})}{in_{max} - in_{min}} + out_{min}$ | $x$ = input, $y$ = scaled output |
| **Baud Rate (Serial)** | Bits per second (e.g., **9600 bps** standard) | — |
| **High/Low Logic Voltages (TTL)** | $V_{IH} \geq 3.0\,\text{V}$ ; $V_{IL} \leq 1.5\,\text{V}$ | For 5 V CMOS logic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TYPOGRAPHY RULE:** Absolute-value bars and conditional dividers in formulas are rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 1024 \right\rfloor$) — never raw `|` — to prevent breaking the KTU-style markdown rendering.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Arduino programming underpins virtually every **rapid-prototyping** workflow in modern engineering:
- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil-moisture sensor (analog) → decision logic → relay-driven water pump.
- **Wearable Health:** Pulse sensor (analog) → threshold detection → buzzer/LED alert.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Vibration sensor (digital interrupt) → counter → cloud dashboard via ESP8266/ESP32.
- **Home Automation:** PIR motion sensor → servo/relay control → MQTT publish to phone.

The simplicity of Arduino's `setup()` + `loop()` model democratises embedded development, allowing mechanical, civil, and biomedical engineers to integrate sensing intelligence without mastering the steep learning curve of bare-metal register programming.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Anatomy of an Arduino Sketch — Exhaustive Walkthrough

Every Arduino sketch (file with extension `.ino`) consists of **two mandatory functions** plus any auxiliary code, comments, libraries, or user-defined functions. The IDE auto-generates a function prototype for `loop()` and `setup()` if you don't, but it is best practice to declare them yourself.

**Canonical Skeleton:**

```cpp
/* ============================================================
   File        : MyFirstSketch.ino
   Board       : Arduino UNO R3 (ATmega328P, 16 MHz)
   Description : Blinks the built-in LED (Pin 13) every 1 s
   ============================================================ */

// ---------- 1. LIBRARY INCLUDES ----------
#include <Arduino.h>   // Core definitions (auto-included, but shown for clarity)

// ---------- 2. CONSTANT DECLARATIONS ----------
const int LED_PIN = 13;        // Built-in LED on every UNO board
const unsigned long BLINK_MS = 1000UL;  // 1-second interval

// ---------- 3. GLOBAL VARIABLES ----------
int blinkCount = 0;            // Counter survives across loop() iterations

// ---------- 4. SETUP FUNCTION (runs ONCE at power-on/reset) ----------
void setup() {
    pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);              // Configure pin 13 as output
    Serial.begin(9600);                    // Initialise UART at 9600 bps
    Serial.println("System Initialised");  // Diagnostic message
}

// ---------- 5. LOOP FUNCTION (runs FOREVER after setup) ----------
void loop() {
    digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH);           // Drive pin HIGH → LED ON
    Serial.println("LED ON");
    delay(BLINK_MS);                       // Halt CPU for 1000 ms
    digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW);            // Drive pin LOW → LED OFF
    Serial.println("LED OFF");
    delay(BLINK_MS);                       // Halt CPU for 1000 ms
    blinkCount++;                          // Increment persistent counter
}
```

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Explanation of Each Block

**Step 1 — Library Includes (`#include`):** The preprocessor directive pulls in header files before compilation. `Arduino.h` exposes all built-in functions like `pinMode`, `digitalWrite`, and `delay`. The IDE injects it **implicitly**, so explicit inclusion is optional.

**Step 2 — Constants (`const`):** Values placed in **flash memory** (not SRAM) using the `const` qualifier, freeing precious 2 KB of working memory. The `UL` suffix forces an **unsigned long** literal — best practice for `millis()`-based timing.

**Step 3 — Global Variables:** Declared outside any function, these retain their value between iterations of `loop()`. SRAM-allocated, so use sparingly on the UNO.

**Step 4 — `setup()`:** The `void` return type means no value is returned. Runs **exactly once** after every reset, power-on, or wake-from-sleep. Used for **initialisation** — pin modes, serial ports, sensor calibration, attach interrupts.

**Step 5 — `loop()`:** The infinite heartbeat. After `setup()` completes, the MCU jumps here and **never leaves** until power is cut. Inside, you implement your state machine, periodic reads, and control logic.

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation — ADC Reading to Voltage

Given the analog input pins of the ATmega328P use a **successive-approximation 10-bit ADC**, we now derive the conversion of a raw digital count back to engineering units.

**Given:**
- $D$ = digital reading from `analogRead(pin)` → integer in [0, 1023]
- $V_{ref}$ = 5.0 V (default reference)
- $n$ = 10 (number of bits)

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Total quantisation levels} &\quad L = 2^{n} = 2^{10} = 1024 \\
\text{Step 2: Size of each step (LSB voltage)} &\quad \Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{L} = \dfrac{5.0}{1024} = 4.8828125 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} \\
\text{Step 3: Reconstruct the input voltage} &\quad V_{in} = D \times \Delta V \\
\text{Step 4: Substitute} &\quad V_{in} = D \times \dfrac{5.0}{1024} \\
\text{Step 5: Simplify (multiplier form)} &\quad V_{in} = \dfrac{D}{1023} \times 5.0 \;\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The denominator in Step 5 uses **1023** (not 1024) because the ADC maps the maximum **5 V** to the count **1023**, leaving 0 V mapped to 0. This is the canonical form implemented in `map()` and is the form examiners expect you to write.

**Numerical Example:** If `analogRead(A0)` returns **512**, then:

$$
V_{in} = \dfrac{512}{1023} \times 5.0 \approx 2.503\,\text{V}
$$

## 3.3 Mathematical Derivation — PWM Duty to Equivalent DC Voltage

The Arduino's `analogWrite(pin, value)` does **not** produce a true analog voltage. It produces a **Pulse-Width Modulated (PWM)** square wave whose **duty cycle** is:

$$
\text{Duty Cycle (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{value}}{255} \times 100
$$

The **average DC equivalent** delivered to a slow-responding load (e.g., a motor or LED filtered by the eye) is:

$$
V_{avg} = \dfrac{\text{value}}{255} \times V_{CC} = \dfrac{\text{value}}{255} \times 5.0
$$

**Numerical Example:** `analogWrite(9, 128)` produces:

$$
V_{avg} = \dfrac{128}{255} \times 5.0 \approx 2.51\,\text{V}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Core Built-in Functions

| Function | Signature | Purpose | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `pinMode(pin, mode)` | `void pinMode(uint8_t pin, uint8_t mode)` | Set pin as `INPUT`, `OUTPUT`, or `INPUT_PULLUP` | Configure LED pin in `setup()` |
| `digitalWrite(pin, value)` | `void digitalWrite(uint8_t pin, uint8_t val)` | Drive pin `HIGH` or `LOW` | Turn LED/motor on/off |
| `digitalRead(pin)` | `int digitalRead(uint8_t pin)` | Returns `HIGH` or `LOW` | Read push-button state |
| `analogRead(pin)` | `int analogRead(uint8_t pin)` | Returns 0–1023 | Read LDR, potentiometer, temperature sensor |
| `analogWrite(pin, value)` | `void analogWrite(uint8_t pin, int val)` | PWM 0–255 | LED brightness, motor speed |
| `delay(ms)` | `void delay(unsigned long ms)` | Blocking pause in ms | Simple timing |
| `millis()` | `unsigned long millis(void)` | Milliseconds since boot | Non-blocking timing |
| `Serial.begin(baud)` | `void Serial.begin(long baud)` | Init UART | Debug output |
| `Serial.print(data)` | various overloads | Print to UART | Logging, debugging |
| `attachInterrupt(pin, ISR, mode)` | `void attachInterrupt(uint8_t, void(*)(void), int)` | React to pin change | Rotary encoders, emergency stops |

## 3.5 Control Structures in Arduino

Since Arduino inherits C/C++ control flow, the following constructs are available:

- **Conditional:** `if`, `else if`, `else`, `switch … case … default`
- **Looping:** `for`, `while`, `do … while`
- **Jumping:** `break`, `continue`, `return`, `goto` (discouraged)

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Blocking Timing (Industry Best Practice):** Avoid `delay()` in production IoT firmware because it halts the CPU. Use `millis()`-based scheduling:
> ```cpp
> unsigned long previousMillis = 0;
> const long interval = 1000;
> void loop() {
>     unsigned long currentMillis = millis();
>     if (currentMillis - previousMillis >= interval) {
>         previousMillis = currentMillis;
>         // Do periodic task here
>     }
>     // CPU free for other tasks
> }
> ```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Arduino Program Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On or Reset] --> B[Initialise Global Variables]
    B --> C[Run setup Function ONCE]
    C --> D[Begin Infinite Loop]
    D --> E[Execute loop Function]
    E --> F{Read Sensors}
    F --> G[Process Data with Control Logic]
    G --> H[Update Actuators and Outputs]
    H --> I[Handle Serial and Interrupts]
    I --> J[Small delay to stabilise]
    J --> D
```

## 4.2 Arduino IDE Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Developer writes sketch in IDE Editor] --> B[Verify with Ctrl plus R]
    B --> C{Compilation Errors?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Fix code in Editor]
    D --> B
    C -- No --> E[Generate HEX file in tmp folder]
    E --> F[Connect Board via USB]
    F --> G[Upload with Ctrl plus U]
    G --> H[AVRDUDE sends HEX to Bootloader]
    H --> I[Board resets and runs new sketch]
    I --> J[Serial Monitor shows output]
```

## 4.3 Arduino UNO R3 Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PWR[Power Section]
        P1[Barrel Jack 7 to 12 V] --> P2[5 V Regulator LM1117]
        P3[USB 5 V] --> P2
        P2 --> P4[3.3 V LDO Regulator]
        P2 --> P5[5 V Rail to MCU and Pins]
    end
    subgraph MCU[ATmega328P Microcontroller]
        M1[16 MHz Crystal Oscillator]
        M2[AVR ALU 8 bit]
        M3[32 KB Flash]
        M4[2 KB SRAM]
        M5[1 KB EEPROM]
        M6[10 bit ADC 6 channels]
        M7[UART SPI I2C]
    end
    subgraph IO[Pin Headers]
        I1[Digital 0 to 13] --> I2[6 PWM 3 5 6 9 10 11]
        I3[Analog A0 to A5] --> I4[10 bit ADC input]
        I5[Power GND 5V 3V3 VIN]
    end
    P5 --> MCU
    MCU --> IO
    USB[USB Type B] --> MCU
```

## 4.4 Sensor-to-Actuator Mapping Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Temperature Sensor LM35] --> A1[Analog Pin A0]
    S2[LDR Light Sensor] --> A2[Analog Pin A1]
    S3[Push Button] --> A3[Digital Pin 2]
    S4[PIR Motion Sensor] --> A4[Digital Pin 3]
    A1 --> M1[MCU reads ADC value]
    A2 --> M1
    A3 --> M1
    A4 --> M1
    M1 --> L1{Decision Logic}
    L1 -->|Temp too high| O1[Buzzer on Pin 8]
    L1 -->|Darkness detected| O2[LED on Pin 9]
    L1 -->|Motion detected| O3[Motor on Pin 10]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node identifiers are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`, `M1`, `I3`) and labels are plain uppercase text to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Mermaid safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model]`
**Explain the difference between `setup()` and `loop()` functions in an Arduino sketch. Why are both mandatory?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `setup()` is executed **exactly once** at the start of the program (after reset/power-on). It is used for **initialisation** of pin modes, serial communication, libraries, and sensor calibration. (1.5 marks)
- `loop()` is executed **repeatedly and infinitely** after `setup()` finishes. It contains the main logic of the program such as reading sensors, decision-making, and controlling actuators. (1.5 marks)
- **Why mandatory:** Arduino's runtime architecture *requires* an entry point and a continuous execution engine; without them, the MCU would have no defined behaviour after reset.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model]`
**What is the difference between `digitalRead()` and `analogRead()` functions? Mention the pin types and return ranges.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `digitalRead(pin)` reads a discrete binary state from a **digital pin** (D0–D13) and returns `HIGH` (5 V) or `LOW` (0 V). (1.5 marks)
- `analogRead(pin)` reads a continuous voltage from an **analog pin** (A0–A5) using the 10-bit ADC and returns an integer in the range **0 to 1023**, where 0 corresponds to 0 V and 1023 corresponds to 5 V. (1.5 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Every Part B question carries **14 marks** and must have sub-parts (typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks). Internal choice means you can answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part below is worth **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 / Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat sketch, explain the structure of an Arduino program. List and briefly describe any four built-in functions used for digital I/O.**

**Model Solution:**

The Arduino program (called a *sketch*) has the following structure:

1. **Comment Header** — description, author, date.
2. **Library Inclusions** — `#include <Library.h>`.
3. **Constant Declarations** — using `const` keyword.
4. **Global Variables** — declared outside functions.
5. **`setup()` Function** — runs once, used for initialisation.
6. **`loop()` Function** — runs forever, contains main logic.

**Four Built-in Digital I/O Functions:**

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| `pinMode(pin, mode)` | Configures the specified pin as `INPUT`, `OUTPUT`, or `INPUT_PULLUP`. |
| `digitalWrite(pin, value)` | Sets a digital pin to `HIGH` (5 V) or `LOW` (0 V). |
| `digitalRead(pin)` | Reads the state of a digital pin and returns `HIGH` or `LOW`. |
| `delay(ms)` | Pauses program execution for the specified number of milliseconds. |

*(Marks distribution: structure explanation = 3 marks, four functions table = 4 marks.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write an Arduino program to read the value from a potentiometer connected to A0 and display the corresponding voltage (0–5 V) on the Serial Monitor.**

**Model Solution:**

```cpp
const int POT_PIN = A0;
float voltage = 0.0;

void setup() {
    Serial.begin(9600);                // Initialise serial at 9600 bps
    pinMode(POT_PIN, INPUT);           // Configure as input
}

void loop() {
    int raw = analogRead(POT_PIN);     // Read 0–1023
    voltage = (raw / 1023.0) * 5.0;    // Convert to voltage
    Serial.print("ADC: ");
    Serial.print(raw);
    Serial.print("  Voltage: ");
    Serial.print(voltage);
    Serial.println(" V");
    delay(500);                        // Update twice per second
}
```

**Explanation of key steps:**
- `analogRead(A0)` returns an integer between 0 and 1023.
- The formula $V_{in} = \dfrac{D}{1023} \times 5.0$ converts it to voltage.
- `Serial.print` displays on the IDE's Serial Monitor.
- `delay(500)` ensures readability without flooding the UART buffer.

*(Marks distribution: program syntax = 3 marks, correct ADC-to-voltage formula = 2 marks, output display logic = 2 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model]` *(Alternative Choice)*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the data types supported by Arduino with examples. Why is the choice of data type critical for an 8-bit MCU with only 2 KB of SRAM?**

**Model Solution:**

| Data Type | Size (bytes) | Range / Precision | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `void` | 0 | No value | Used in function returns |
| `boolean` | 1 | `true` / `false` (0 or 1) | `boolean ledState = true;` |
| `char` | 1 | -128 to +127 (ASCII) | `char letter = 'A';` |
| `unsigned char` / `byte` | 1 | 0 to 255 | `byte data = 0xFF;` |
| `int` | 2 | -32,768 to 32,767 | `int sensorValue = 512;` |
| `unsigned int` | 2 | 0 to 65,535 | `unsigned int count = 1000;` |
| `long` | 4 | -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647 | `long timestamp;` |
| `unsigned long` | 4 | 0 to 4,294,967,295 | `unsigned long t = millis();` |
| `float` | 4 | ±3.4028235E+38 (6–7 digit precision) | `float voltage = 3.3;` |
| `double` | 4 (on UNO) | Same as `float` | `double pi = 3.14;` |

**Why critical for an 8-bit MCU:**
- The ATmega328P has only **2 KB of SRAM**; every byte matters.
- Using `float` when `int` suffices wastes 2 bytes per variable.
- `int` on UNO is **16 bits** (2 bytes), not the 32 bits of desktop machines — common student mistake.
- Global/heap exhaustion causes silent crashes or boot loops.

*(Marks distribution: table = 4 marks, justification of memory constraints = 3 marks.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write an Arduino program to fade an LED connected to Pin 9 (PWM) in and out continuously using a `for` loop. Mention the function used and the relevant pin characteristic.**

**Model Solution:**

```cpp
const int LED_PIN = 9;          // Pin 9 supports PWM (~ on UNO)
int brightness = 0;             // 0–255
int fadeStep = 5;               // Step size

void setup() {
    pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);
}

void loop() {
    // Fade IN from 0 to 255
    for (brightness = 0; brightness <= 255; brightness += fadeStep) {
        analogWrite(LED_PIN, brightness);
        delay(30);              // Allow human eye to perceive change
    }
    // Fade OUT from 255 to 0
    for (brightness = 255; brightness >= 0; brightness -= fadeStep) {
        analogWrite(LED_PIN, brightness);
        delay(30);
    }
}
```

**Relevant Pin Characteristic:** Pin 9 is a **PWM-capable** pin on the Arduino UNO, indicated by the `~` symbol next to the pin number on the board. The `analogWrite()` function modulates a digital pin's duty cycle in the range **0–255** to approximate an analog output. The default PWM frequency on pin 9 is approximately **490 Hz**.

*(Marks distribution: program = 4 marks, function and pin characteristic = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Wrong ADC denominator** — Examiners **strictly** deduct 1 mark if you use 1024 instead of 1023 in the voltage conversion formula $V_{in} = \dfrac{D}{1023} \times V_{ref}$. Memorise this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting `void` keyword** — Both `setup()` and `loop()` must begin with `void`. Missing it loses 0.5 marks.
> 3. **Confusing digital with analog pins** — `analogWrite()` works **only** on PWM pins (3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11). Writing it for pin 7 is a silent error in some IDEs.
> 4. **Not closing Serial Monitor** — Open the Serial Monitor at the **correct baud rate (9600)** or output will appear as garbage characters.
> 5. **Missing semicolon `;`** — Triggers compilation error; remember each statement ends with one.
> 6. **Confusing `=` with `==`** — `=` is assignment, `==` is comparison. Writing `if (x = 5)` instead of `if (x == 5)` is a **classic deduction trap** worth 1 mark.
> 7. **Uninitialised pin state** — Always set `pinMode()` in `setup()`; floating inputs produce erratic readings.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Introduction to Arduino Programming):**

- ✅ Arduino is an **open-source platform** combining a microcontroller board, IDE, and C/C++-based language.
- ✅ The **ATmega328P** runs at **16 MHz** with **32 KB flash, 2 KB SRAM, 1 KB EEPROM**.
- ✅ **Every sketch has two mandatory functions:** `void setup()` and `void loop()`.
- ✅ `pinMode(pin, OUTPUT)` is mandatory in `setup()` before any `digitalWrite()`.
- ✅ `digitalRead()` returns `HIGH`/`LOW`; `analogRead()` returns **0–1023**.
- ✅ The ADC formula is $V_{in} = \dfrac{D}{1023} \times 5.0\,\text{V}$ (denominator is **1023**, not 1024).
- ✅ `analogWrite(pin, value)` uses **PWM** and works only on pins **3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11**; range is **0–255**.
- ✅ `delay(ms)` is **blocking** — it halts the CPU. Use `millis()` for non-blocking timing.
- ✅ `Serial.begin(9600)` opens the UART; always match the **Serial Monitor baud rate**.
- ✅ Arduino UNO's `int` is **16-bit (2 bytes)**, not 32-bit; `float` consumes 4 bytes — use sparingly.
- ✅ **Six** analog input pins: A0–A5. **Fourteen** digital pins: D0–D13 (of which **6** are PWM).
- ✅ The **bootloader** (Optiboot) is a small pre-loaded program that accepts code via USB/UART — no external programmer needed.
- ✅ Always close curly braces `{}` and end statements with `;` — most compilation errors stem from these.
- ✅ Avoid using **pins 0 and 1** as GPIO when USB serial is active, as they are shared with the UART.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration of Sensors and Actuators with Arduino
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT612 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Integration of Sensors and Actuators with Arduino # 📘 Module 3 — Integration of Sensors and Actuators with Arduino ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Sensor–Actuator Integration with Arduino** refers to the systematic process of interfacing physical input devices (sensors) and output devices (actuators) with an Arduino microcontroller board through its General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pins, Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) channels, and Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) capable pins, in order to perceive, compute, and act upon environmental variables in an Internet of Things (IoT) system. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, the term *integration* encompasses three distinct engineering layers: 1. **Hardware Layer** — physical wiring, voltage-level compatibility, current-limiting components. 2. **Firmware Layer** — the C/C++ sketch that configures pin modes, reads analog/digital values, and drives outputs. 3. **Signal Layer** — the conversion between physical phenomena (temperature, light, motion) and electrical signals (voltage, current, PWM duty cycle). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of OECPT612 emphasizes the *practical interfacing* of sensors like DHT11, LM35, HC-SR04, LDR, and actuators like DC motors, servo motors, relays, and buzzers. Students must be able to draw circuit-level diagrams, write functional Arduino sketches, and explain ADC/PWM operations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Body and Brain" Model Imagine the human body: - **Sensors** are the *senses* (eyes see light, skin feels temperature, ears hear sound). They convert real-world stimuli into nerve signals. - **Actuators** are the *muscles and limbs* that produce physical responses (hand pulls away from heat, leg steps on a brake). - **Arduino** is the *brain and spinal cord* — it receives the nerve signals, processes them, and decides which muscle to activate. Just as the brain cannot directly "see" light but only interprets electrical impulses from the retina, Arduino cannot directly measure temperature — it only reads a **voltage** (0–5 V) from a temperature sensor and converts that voltage into a number (0–1023) using its internal **10-bit ADC**. > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Definitions for the Examiner:** > - A **sensor** is a *transducer* that converts a physical quantity into an electrical signal. > - An **actuator** is a *transducer* that converts an electrical signal into a physical action. > - Arduino's ADC resolution = **10 bits**, mapping **0 V → 0** and **5 V → 1023**. > - Arduino's PWM frequency on most pins = **~490 Hz**, with 8-bit duty cycle resolution (0–255). ### 1.3 The Arduino Ecosystem — Board Variants | Board | MCU | Digital I/O | Analog Inputs | PWM Pins | Flash | Common Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Arduino Uno R3** | ATmega328P | 14 (6 PWM) | 6 | 6 | 32 KB | Education, prototyping | | **Arduino Mega 2560** | ATmega2560 | 54 (15 PWM) | 16 | 15 | 256 KB | Multi-sensor hubs | | **Arduino Nano** | ATmega328P | 14 (6 PWM) | 8 | 6 | 32 KB | Compact/embedded | | **Arduino Due** | SAM3X8E | 54 (12 PWM) | 12 | 12 | 512 KB | High-speed DSP | > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Tip:** KTU questions almost always default to **Arduino Uno R3** unless specified. Memorize its pin map: `A0–A5` for analog, digital pins `3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11` for PWM (marked with `~`). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three-Stage Signal Pipeline Every sensor-actuator integration in Arduino follows a universal three-stage pipeline: **Stage 1 — Sensing (Transduction + Conditioning)** - The physical quantity (heat, light, distance) is transduced into an analog voltage. - Optional signal conditioning: amplification (e.g., LM35 outputs only 10 mV/°C, may need Op-Amp gain), filtering (RC low-pass to remove 50 Hz noise). **Stage 2 — Processing (ADC + Firmware Logic)** - The analog voltage enters the Arduino's **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC**. - The ADC produces a digital integer between 0 and 1023. - Firmware uses `analogRead()` to capture this integer, then maps it to engineering units (°C, cm, lux). **Stage 3 — Actuation (PWM / Digital Output + Driver)** - The Arduino outputs either a HIGH/LOW digital signal (5 V / 0 V) or a PWM waveform. - High-power actuators (motors, solenoids) are driven through a **driver circuit** (transistor, MOSFET, relay, or H-bridge like L298N) because Arduino pins can source/sink only **40 mA** (absolute max), and **20 mA continuously recommended**. ## 2.2 Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) — The Core Mathematics Arduino's ATmega328P uses a **10-bit Successive Approximation ADC** with a reference voltage $V_{ref}$. **Step 1: ADC Quantization** The digital output code $D$ is given by: $$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1) \right\rfloor$$ For Arduino Uno: $n = 10$, $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V (default), so: $$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{5.0} \times 1023 \right\rfloor$$ **Step 2: Voltage Reconstruction** Given a digital reading $D$, the original input voltage is reconstructed as: $$V_{in} = \frac{D}{1023} \times V_{ref}$$ **Step 3: Sensor Calibration (Engineering Units Conversion)** For a linear sensor with sensitivity $S$ (V/unit) and offset $V_0$: $$\text{Physical Value} = \frac{V_{in} - V_0}{S}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Resolution is the smallest detectable voltage change: $$\Delta V_{min} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5.0}{1024} \approx 4.88 \text{ mV per LSB}$$ ## 2.3 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) for Actuator Control PWM is a digital technique that simulates an analog output by rapidly switching a pin HIGH and LOW. The **duty cycle** determines the average voltage delivered to the actuator. **Average Voltage Delivered:** $$V_{avg} = D_{cycle} \times V_{CC} = \frac{t_{on}}{T} \times V_{CC}$$ Where $T = t_{on} + t_{off}$ is the period. **Arduino Mapping:** $$\text{analogWrite}(\text{pin}, x) \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_{cycle} = \frac{x}{255} \times 100\%$$ So `analogWrite(pin, 128)` gives a 50.2% duty cycle. ## 2.4 Sensor-Specific Conversion Formulas | Sensor | Physical Quantity | Output Type | Conversion Formula | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | **LM35** | Temperature | Analog (10 mV/°C) | $T(°C) = \frac{V_{in}}{0.01}$ | Range: 0–150 °C | | **TMP36** | Temperature | Analog (10 mV/°C, 500 mV offset) | $T(°C) = 100 \times (V_{in} - 0.5)$ | Range: −40 to +125 °C | | **DHT11** | Temp + Humidity | Digital (single-wire) | Library-based `dht.readHumidity()` | 0–50 °C, 20–90% RH | | **HC-SR04** | Distance | Digital (trigger/echo) | $d(cm) = \frac{t_{echo}(\mu s)}{58.2}$ | Range: 2–400 cm | | **LDR (Voltage Divider)** | Light Intensity | Analog | $R_{LDR} = R_{fixed} \times \frac{1023 - D}{D}$ | Inverse relation | | **MQ-2** | Gas Concentration | Analog + Digital | $V_{out} \propto \text{ppm (non-linear)}$ | Needs warm-up ~20 s | | **PIR (HC-SR501)** | Motion | Digital (HIGH/LOW) | Boolean — no formula | 3–7 m range | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Revision Table | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | ADC Digital Code | $D = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 1023$ | Dimensionless integer | | 2 | Voltage Reconstruction | $V_{in} = \dfrac{D}{1023} \times V_{ref}$ | Volts | | 3 | ADC Resolution | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{10}}$ | ≈ 4.88 mV | | 4 | PWM Duty Cycle | $D_c = \dfrac{t_{on}}{T} \times 100\%$ | Percent (0–100%) | | 5 | PWM Average Voltage | $V_{avg} = D_c \times V_{CC}$ | Volts | | 6 | `analogWrite` Mapping | $x = D_c \times 255$ | Integer 0–255 | | 7 | LM35 Temperature | $T = \dfrac{V_{in}}{0.01}$ | °C (10 mV/°C) | | 8 | TMP36 Temperature | $T = 100 \times (V_{in} - 0.5)$ | °C | | 9 | HC-SR04 Distance | $d = \dfrac{t_{echo}}{58.2}$ | Centimeters | | 10 | Ultrasonic Speed of Sound | $v = 0.034 \text{ cm/µs}$ (at 20°C) | cm/µs | | 11 | Voltage Divider Output | $V_{out} = V_{in} \times \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Volts | | 12 | LDR Resistance | $R_{LDR} = R_{ref} \times \dfrac{1023 - D}{D}$ | Ohms | | 13 | Servo Angle (Arduino) | `myservo.write(deg)` | Degrees 0–180 | | 14 | Relay Coil Current | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_{coil}}$ | Amperes (typ. 70 mA) | | 15 | H-Bridge Motor Voltage | $V_{motor} = V_{supply} - 2V_{D(S)}$ | Volts (L298N drop ≈ 1.4–2 V) | > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing `analogRead()` (returns 0–1023) with `analogWrite()` (accepts 0–255). The factor of 4 is **not** linear — it is $2^{10}/2^{8} = 4$ because the ADC is 10-bit and PWM is 8-bit. When mapping, use the `map()` function correctly: `map(value, 0, 1023, 0, 255)`. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — LM35 Temperature Sensor Reading **Problem:** An LM35 temperature sensor is connected to Arduino Uno's analog pin A0. The `analogRead()` returns a value of **307**. Calculate the temperature in °C and the equivalent voltage at the pin. **Given:** - $D = 307$ - $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V - $n = 10$ bits - LM35 sensitivity = **10 mV/°C** = **0.01 V/°C** ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Reconstruct the input voltage from the ADC code.** We use the voltage reconstruction formula: $$V_{in} = \frac{D}{2^n - 1} \times V_{ref}$$ Substituting the known values: $$V_{in} = \frac{307}{1023} \times 5.0$$ Computing the ratio: $$V_{in} = 0.3001 \times 5.0$$ $$V_{in} = 1.5005 \text{ V} \approx 1.50 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2: Convert voltage to temperature using LM35's sensitivity.** The LM35 outputs exactly 10 mV per °C with zero offset at 0 °C: $$T(°C) = \frac{V_{in}}{S} = \frac{V_{in}}{0.01 \text{ V/°C}}$$ Substituting: $$T(°C) = \frac{1.5005}{0.01}$$ $$T(°C) = 150.05 \text{ °C}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** The sensor is reporting approximately **150 °C**, which is at the upper limit of the LM35's range (0–150 °C). The board may be in a high-temperature environment, or the sensor may be faulty. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Distance Measurement **Problem:** An HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor is triggered by Arduino. The `pulseIn(echoPin, HIGH)` function returns a time of **1746 µs**. Calculate the distance to the obstacle in **centimeters** and **inches**. Also verify whether the reading is within the sensor's valid range. **Given:** - $t_{echo} = 1746$ µs - Speed of sound at 20 °C: $v = 343$ m/s = **0.0343 cm/µs** - HC-SR04 range: 2 cm to 400 cm ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Understand the physics of the echo.** The ultrasonic pulse travels to the obstacle and **back**, so the total distance traveled by the sound wave is: $$d_{total} = v \times t_{echo}$$ The **one-way distance** to the obstacle is half of the round-trip distance: $$d = \frac{v \times t_{echo}}{2}$$ **Step 2: Substitute the values.** $$d = \frac{0.0343 \text{ cm/µs} \times 1746 \text{ µs}}{2}$$ First, compute the numerator: $$0.0343 \times 1746 = 59.89 \text{ cm}$$ Now divide by 2: $$d = \frac{59.89}{2} = 29.95 \text{ cm} \approx 30 \text{ cm}$$ **Step 3: Convert to inches (1 inch = 2.54 cm).** $$d_{inches} = \frac{30}{2.54} = 11.81 \text{ inches}$$ **Step 4: Validate against sensor range.** $$2 \text{ cm} \leq 30 \text{ cm} \leq 400 \text{ cm} \quad \checkmark$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Compact Formula Derivation:** > The standard formula $d = \frac{t}{58.2}$ (for cm) is derived as: > > $$d = \frac{0.0343}{2} \times t = 0.01715 \times t \quad (\text{simplified: } d = t/58.31)$$ > > Different sources cite 58.2 (rounded) or 29.1 (for one-way using round-trip time in µs at 0 °C). Always use $d = t/58$ for a clean approximation at room temperature. ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — PWM LED Brightness Control **Problem:** Arduino is driving an LED through a transistor on PWM pin 9. The code executes `analogWrite(9, 192)`. Calculate the **duty cycle**, the **average voltage** across the LED (assume $V_{CC} = 5$ V), and the **effective current** if the LED has a series resistor of $R = 220$ Ω and forward voltage $V_f = 2.0$ V. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Calculate the duty cycle from the `analogWrite` value.** The Arduino `analogWrite()` function uses 8-bit PWM, so the maximum value is $2^8 - 1 = 255$: $$D_c = \frac{x}{255} \times 100\% = \frac{192}{255} \times 100\%$$ $$D_c = 0.7529 \times 100\% = 75.29\%$$ **Step 2: Calculate the average voltage across the LED-resistor branch.** $$V_{avg} = D_c \times V_{CC} = 0.7529 \times 5.0$$ $$V_{avg} = 3.765 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3: Calculate the effective current through the LED.** Using Ohm's Law on the series branch (subtracting the LED's forward voltage drop): $$V_R = V_{avg} - V_f = 3.765 - 2.0 = 1.765 \text{ V}$$ $$I_{LED} = \frac{V_R}{R} = \frac{1.765}{220} = 8.02 \text{ mA}$$ **Step 4: Compare with full-on state.** If `analogWrite(9, 255)` were used, $V_{avg} = 5.0$ V, $V_R = 3.0$ V, giving $I_{max} = 13.6$ mA. The LED is therefore at: $$\frac{8.02}{13.6} \times 100\% = 58.97\% \text{ of full brightness}$$ > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** The human eye perceives brightness **logarithmically**, not linearly. A 75% duty cycle does *not* appear 75% as bright — it appears roughly 90% as bright. This is why LED dimming often uses a corrected gamma curve in professional applications. ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — LDR Light Sensor Voltage Divider **Problem:** A voltage divider is built with an LDR (top, $R_1$) and a fixed resistor $R_2 = 10$ kΩ (bottom). The Arduino's A1 pin reads `analogRead() = 760`. Calculate the **LDR's resistance** and infer the **light condition**. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Reconstruct the voltage at the divider midpoint.** $$V_{out} = \frac{D}{1023} \times V_{ref} = \frac{760}{1023} \times 5.0$$ $$V_{out} = 0.7430 \times 5.0 = 3.715 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2: Apply the voltage divider equation.** For a divider with $R_1$ on top and $R_2$ on bottom: $$V_{out} = V_{in} \times \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ Rearranging to solve for $R_1$ (the LDR): $$R_1 + R_2 = \frac{V_{in} \times R_2}{V_{out}}$$ $$R_1 = \frac{V_{in} \times R_2}{V_{out}} - R_2$$ $$R_1 = \frac{5.0 \times 10000}{3.715} - 10000$$ $$R_1 = 13458.9 - 10000 = 3458.9 \text{ Ω} \approx 3.46 \text{ kΩ}$$ **Step 3: Interpret the light condition.** LDR resistance **decreases** as light **increases**. A 3.46 kΩ reading indicates **bright light** (typical LDRs are 1 MΩ in darkness and a few hundred ohms in direct sunlight). ## 3.5 Summary — Analytical Decision Matrix | Scenario | Recommended Pin Type | Driver Needed? | Code Construct | |---|---|---|---| | Read a potentiometer | Analog Input (A0–A5) | No | `analogRead()` | | Blink an LED | Digital Output | Resistor only | `digitalWrite()` | | Dim an LED | PWM Output (pin ~) | MOSFET if high-power | `analogWrite()` | | Read a push button | Digital Input | Pull-down/up resistor | `digitalRead()` | | Read DHT11 | Any Digital | None | Library `dht.readTemperature()` | | Drive DC motor | PWM + Direction pins | **L298N H-bridge** | `analogWrite()` + `digitalWrite()` | | Drive servo | PWM (pin 9, 10) | Optional external 5V | `Servo.h` library | | Switch AC appliance | Digital Output | **Relay module** | `digitalWrite(HIGH)` | | Read ultrasonic | Trig + Echo (2 digital) | Voltage divider on echo if 5V | `pulseIn()` | | Read gas (MQ-2) | Analog Input | 5V warm-up, load resistor | `analogRead()` | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — Sensor → Arduino → Actuator Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR Env[Physical Environment] -->|Stimulus| SensorBlock SensorBlock[Sensor Module
Transducer + Signal Conditioning] -->|Analog Voltage 0-5V| ADC ADC[Arduino ADC
10-bit SAR
0 to 1023] -->|Digital Code D| MCU MCU[ATmega328P
Firmware Logic
threshold compare map] -->|Decision| DriverSel{Output Type?} DriverSel -->|PWM Signal| PWMPin[PWM Pin 3 5 6 9 10 11] DriverSel -->|Digital HIGH LOW| DigiPin[Digital Pin 0-13] PWMPin --> Driver1[MOSFET or L298N] DigiPin --> Driver2[Relay or Transistor] Driver1 --> Actuator1[DC Motor or LED Array] Driver2 --> Actuator2[Solenoid Relay Buzzer] Actuator1 --> Action1[Physical Action 1] Actuator2 --> Action2[Physical Action 2] MCU -.->|Serial UART TX RX| Cloud[Serial Monitor or WiFi Module] Cloud -->|Optional IoT Upload| Cloud2[ThingSpeak Blynk MQTT] ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Pin Assignment Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ArduinoUno[Arduino Uno R3 Board] PWR[Power Section
5V 3.3V GND VIN] AN[Analog Pins A0 to A5
ADC Channels] DIG[Digital Pins D0 to D13] PWM[PWM Capable Pins
D3 D5 D6 D9 D10 D11] UART[Serial UART
D0 RX D1 TX] I2C[I2C Bus
A4 SDA A5 SCL] SPI[SPI Bus
D10 SS D11 MOSI D12 MISO D13 SCK] end subgraph SensorBank[Sensor Bank] S1[LM35 Temperature
A0] S2[LDR Light Sensor
A1 Voltage Divider] S3[MQ2 Gas Sensor
A2] S4[DHT11 Humidity
D2 Digital] S5[HC-SR04 Ultrasonic
D7 Trig D8 Echo] S6[PIR Motion
D4 Interrupt Capable] end subgraph ActuatorBank[Actuator Bank] A1[LED Indicator
D9 PWM via Resistor] A2[DC Motor via L298N
D5 PWM D6 D8 Direction] A3[Servo Motor SG90
D10 PWM] A4[Buzzer
D3 PWM] A5[Relay Module 5V Coil
D12 Digital] end S1 --> AN S2 --> AN S3 --> AN S4 --> DIG S5 --> DIG S6 --> DIG PWM --> A1 PWM --> A2 PWM --> A3 PWM --> A4 DIG --> A5 ``` ## 4.3 Generic Arduino Sketch Execution Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Power On or Reset] --> B[Init Hardware Peripherals] B --> C[pinMode INPUT OUTPUT] C --> D[Attach Interrupts if any] D --> E[Initialize Sensors and Libraries] E --> F[Begin Serial 9600 baud] F --> G{loop function} G --> H[Read Sensor 1 analogRead digitalRead] H --> I[Process Data map filter threshold] I --> J[Decision Logic if else switch] J -->|Actuator ON| K[analogWrite or digitalWrite HIGH] J -->|Actuator OFF| L[analogWrite 0 or digitalWrite LOW] K --> M[Delay or Non-blocking Timer] L --> M M --> N[Optional Serial Print Debug] N --> G ``` ## 4.4 Voltage Divider for LDR — Functional Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR V5[5V from Arduino] --> R1[LDR
R1 Variable] R1 --> NodeA[Midpoint Node A] NodeA --> R2[Fixed Resistor
R2 10kΩ] R2 --> GND[Arduino GND] NodeA --> A0[Arduino Analog Pin A0] A0 -.->|ADC reads voltage| MCU[ATmega328P ADC] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Mermaid diagrams:** All node identifiers use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `ArduinoUno`, `SensorBank`) and labels contain raw text only — no bold, italics, or markdown formatting inside quoted labels — to ensure clean Mermaid rendering as required by KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10. ## 4.5 Driver Circuit for High-Current DC Motor ```mermaid flowchart LR Arduino[Arduino PWM Pin D5] --> Gate[G of MOSFET IRF540N] Gate --> GateR[10kΩ Pull-down to GND] Arduino --> Dir1[Arduino D6 to IN1 of L298N] Arduino --> Dir2[Arduino D7 to IN2 of L298N] PWM[Arduino D5 PWM to ENA of L298N] ENA[L298N Enable Pin ENA] --> H[L298N H-Bridge IC] IN1 --> H IN2 --> H H -->|OUT1 OUT2| Motor[DC Motor M] H -->|VS terminal| Vext[External 12V Supply] H -->|GND| GND[Common GND] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define a sensor and an actuator. Give two examples of each and explain the role of an Arduino in interfacing them.** **Model Answer (Expected 3-mark structure):** - **Sensor** (1 mark): A transducer that converts a physical quantity (temperature, light, pressure) into an electrical signal. *Examples:* LM35 (temperature), HC-SR04 (distance). - **Actuator** (1 mark): A transducer that converts an electrical signal into a physical action. *Examples:* DC motor (rotation), relay (switching), buzzer (sound). - **Arduino's Role** (1 mark): Arduino acts as the central processing unit — it uses its ADC to read analog sensor values, processes them via firmware, and outputs control signals (digital HIGH/LOW or PWM) to drive actuators, often via driver circuits due to its limited 40 mA pin current. --- ### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the function of the `analogRead()` and `analogWrite()` functions in Arduino. Why are their return values on different scales?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - `analogRead(pin)` (1 mark): Reads the voltage (0–5 V) on an analog pin and returns a 10-bit integer between **0 and 1023**. The mapping is: $D = (V_{in} / 5.0) \times 1023$. - `analogWrite(pin, value)` (1 mark): Outputs a PWM signal on a PWM-capable pin. The `value` is an 8-bit integer between **0 and 255**, where `value/255` is the duty cycle. - **Different Scales** (1 mark): The ADC has **10-bit resolution** ($2^{10} = 1024$ levels) while PWM is **8-bit** ($2^8 = 256$ levels). The ratio is $1024/256 = 4$, hence the apparent scale difference. To convert an ADC reading to PWM, use `map(adcValue, 0, 1023, 0, 255)`. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an Arduino Uno board. List its important specifications relevant to sensor/actuator interfacing. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Interface an **LM35 temperature sensor** and a **DC motor (via L298N driver)** with Arduino Uno. Draw the circuit diagram, write the complete Arduino sketch to read temperature and run the motor only when temperature exceeds **40 °C**, and explain the working. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — Arduino Uno Architecture **Block diagram description (text form):** The Arduino Uno is built around the **ATmega328P** microcontroller. Its architecture consists of: - **Power Section:** USB connector, barrel jack (7–12 V), 5 V and 3.3 V regulators. - **Digital I/O:** 14 pins (D0–D13); each can be `INPUT` or `OUTPUT`. - **PWM Outputs:** 6 pins (D3, D5, D6, D9, D10, D11) marked with `~`. - **Analog Inputs:** 6 channels (A0–A5) connected to internal 10-bit SAR ADC. - **Communication:** UART (D0/D1), I2C (A4/A5), SPI (D10–D13). - **Memory:** 32 KB Flash, 2 KB SRAM, 1 KB EEPROM. **Key Specifications Table (3 marks):** | Specification | Value | |---|---| | MCU | ATmega328P | | Operating Voltage | 5 V | | Input Voltage (recommended) | 7–12 V | | Digital I/O Pins | 14 (6 PWM) | | Analog Input Pins | 6 (10-bit ADC) | | DC Current per I/O Pin | 20 mA (recommended), 40 mA (absolute max) | | Flash Memory | 32 KB (0.5 KB used by bootloader) | | SRAM | 2 KB | | EEPROM | 1 KB | | Clock Speed | 16 MHz | **Why each spec matters for interfacing (2 marks):** - The **40 mA current limit** is why actuators like DC motors *cannot* be driven directly from pins — driver ICs (L298N) are mandatory. - The **10-bit ADC** sets quantization resolution at 4.88 mV/LSB, affecting precision of sensor readings. - The **6 analog pins** limit simultaneous analog sensor count. #### Model Solution for (b) — LM35 + L298N Motor Interfacing **Circuit Diagram (Mermaid rendering):** ```mermaid flowchart LR LM35[LM35 Sensor] -->|Vout to A0| A0[Arduino A0] LM35 -->|GND| GND1[Common GND] LM35 -->|Vs to 5V| V5[Arduino 5V] A0 --> Arduino[Arduino Uno] Arduino -->|D9 PWM to ENA| ENA[L298N ENA] Arduino -->|D8 Dir to IN1| IN1[L298N IN1] Arduino -->|D7 Dir to IN2| IN2[L298N IN2] ENA --> L298N[L298N H-Bridge] IN1 --> L298N IN2 --> L298N L298N -->|OUT1 OUT2| Motor[DC Motor] L298N -->|12V from battery| V12[External 12V Supply] L298N -->|GND| GND2[Common GND] ``` **Complete Arduino Sketch (4 marks):** ```cpp // LM35 + DC Motor Temperature-Triggered Control const int lm35Pin = A0; // LM35 analog output const int enA = 9; // L298N Enable pin (PWM) const int in1 = 8; // L298N Input 1 const int in2 = 7; // L298N Input 2 void setup() { pinMode(lm35Pin, INPUT); pinMode(enA, OUTPUT); pinMode(in1, OUTPUT); pinMode(in2, OUTPUT); Serial.begin(9600); } void loop() { int adcValue = analogRead(lm35Pin); float voltage = (adcValue / 1023.0) * 5.0; float temperatureC = voltage / 0.01; // LM35: 10 mV/°C Serial.print("Temperature: "); Serial.print(temperatureC); Serial.println(" °C"); if (temperatureC > 40.0) { // Motor ON at 80% speed, forward direction digitalWrite(in1, HIGH); digitalWrite(in2, LOW); analogWrite(enA, 204); // 204/255 ≈ 80% duty cycle } else { // Motor OFF (coast) digitalWrite(in1, LOW); digitalWrite(in2, LOW); analogWrite(enA, 0); } delay(500); } ``` **Working Explanation (3 marks):** 1. The LM35 produces **10 mV per °C**. The Arduino's ADC digitizes this voltage into a 0–1023 code. 2. The firmware reconstructs voltage and divides by 0.01 to obtain °C. 3. A threshold of 40 °C is checked every 500 ms. 4. If exceeded, the L298N receives HIGH on IN1, LOW on IN2 (forward direction) and a PWM duty cycle of ~80% on ENA, driving the motor at controlled speed. 5. If the temperature is below 40 °C, all motor control pins are set LOW, allowing the motor to coast to a stop safely. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the working of the **HC-SR04 ultrasonic distance sensor** with Arduino. Derive the distance formula and write a sketch to display the measured distance on the Serial Monitor. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a suitable circuit, explain how an **LDR-based light sensor** and a **relay-controlled lamp** can be integrated with Arduino to automatically switch the lamp ON in darkness. Write the complete sketch. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor **Working Principle (3 marks):** The HC-SR04 emits a 40 kHz ultrasonic burst when its **TRIG** pin receives a 10 µs HIGH pulse. The sound wave travels through air, reflects off an obstacle, and returns to the **ECHO** pin. The ECHO pin stays HIGH for a duration $t$ proportional to the distance. **Derivation of the Distance Formula (2 marks):** Let $v$ = speed of sound ≈ 0.0343 cm/µs (at 20 °C). The wave travels a total round-trip distance: $$d_{round-trip} = v \times t_{echo} \text{ (cm)}$$ The one-way distance to the obstacle is half of that: $$d = \frac{v \times t_{echo}}{2} = \frac{0.0343 \times t_{echo}}{2} = \frac{t_{echo}}{58.2} \text{ cm}$$ **Arduino Sketch (2 marks):** ```cpp const int trigPin = 7; const int echoPin = 8; void setup() { pinMode(trigPin, OUTPUT); pinMode(echoPin, INPUT); Serial.begin(9600); } void loop() { // Send 10 µs trigger pulse digitalWrite(trigPin, LOW); delayMicroseconds(2); digitalWrite(trigPin, HIGH); delayMicroseconds(10); digitalWrite(trigPin, LOW); // Read echo pulse duration long duration = pulseIn(echoPin, HIGH, 30000); // timeout 30 ms float distanceCm = duration / 58.2; float distanceIn = distanceCm / 2.54; Serial.print("Distance: "); Serial.print(distanceCm); Serial.print(" cm | "); Serial.print(distanceIn); Serial.println(" in"); delay(100); } ``` --- #### Model Solution for (b) — LDR + Relay Automatic Lamp Control **Circuit Description (3 marks):** - **LDR + 10 kΩ resistor** form a voltage divider between 5 V and GND. The midpoint connects to **A0**. - The **relay module's IN pin** connects to Arduino **D7**. The relay's VCC and GND connect to 5 V and GND respectively. - The lamp (AC or DC load) is wired through the relay's COM and NO (Normally Open) contacts. The relay provides galvanic isolation, protecting the Arduino from high-voltage spikes. **Arduino Sketch (4 marks):** ```cpp const int ldrPin = A0; const int relayPin = 7; const int threshold = 500; // ADC threshold for darkness void setup() { pinMode(ldrPin, INPUT); pinMode(relayPin, OUTPUT); digitalWrite(relayPin, LOW); // Lamp initially OFF Serial.begin(9600); } void loop() { int ldrValue = analogRead(ldrPin); Serial.print("LDR ADC: "); Serial.println(ldrValue); // LDR resistance increases in darkness → voltage at A0 decreases → ADC value decreases if (ldrValue < threshold) { digitalWrite(relayPin, HIGH); // Energize relay → lamp ON Serial.println("Darkness detected — Lamp ON"); } else { digitalWrite(relayPin, LOW); // De-energize relay → lamp OFF Serial.println("Light detected — Lamp OFF"); } delay(200); } ``` **Working Explanation (text):** In bright light, the LDR's resistance is low (a few hundred ohms), so the voltage at A0 is high (ADC value > 500). The relay remains de-energized and the lamp stays OFF. In darkness, the LDR's resistance rises sharply (hundreds of kΩ), pulling A0's voltage low (ADC < 500). The Arduino sets the relay pin HIGH, energizing the relay coil, closing the COM-NO contact, and switching the lamp ON. Hysteresis (using two different thresholds) can be added to prevent rapid toggling at the boundary. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Sensor/Actuator Questions:** > 1. **Wrong ADC range** — Writing `analogRead()` returns "0 to 1024" instead of **0 to 1023**. (Lose 0.5 mark) > 2. **Confusing `analogWrite` with DAC** — `analogWrite` does NOT produce a true analog voltage; it produces a **PWM square wave**. (Lose 1 mark if confused with DAC output) > 3. **Driving motors directly from Arduino pin** — Arduino pins can source only **40 mA**. A DC motor needs 100–500 mA. Always mention **driver ICs (L298N, BTS7960)** or **transistor/MOSFET** stages. > 4. **Missing common ground** — When using an external supply (12 V battery for motor), students often forget to **tie the external supply's GND to Arduino's GND**. Without common ground, the L298N logic inputs will not register HIGH/LOW correctly. > 5. **Forgetting `pinMode()`** — In `setup()`, every used pin must be configured. Skipping `pinMode(pin, OUTPUT)` for an LED results in no output. > 6. **Using `delay()` in time-critical loops** — For ultrasonic or motion sensors, blocking delays cause missed events. Use `millis()`-based non-blocking timing. > 7. **Wrong LM35 formula** — Writing `temperature = voltage * 100` instead of `voltage / 0.01` (or equivalently `voltage * 100` is correct! Both are valid: $V/0.01 = V \times 100$). Verify your formula matches the sensitivity **10 mV/°C**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist **Arduino Hardware Fundamentals** - [ ] Arduino Uno has **14 digital pins** (6 PWM), **6 analog inputs** (10-bit ADC), 16 MHz clock, 5 V logic. - [ ] Maximum pin current = **40 mA** (absolute max), **20 mA** (continuous recommended). - [ ] PWM pins on Uno: **D3, D5, D6, D9, D10, D11** (marked with `~`). - [ ] I2C pins: **A4 (SDA), A5 (SCL)**. - [ ] SPI pins: **D10 (SS), D11 (MOSI), D12 (MISO), D13 (SCK)**. **ADC & Sensor Reading** - [ ] `analogRead()` returns 0–1023 (10-bit); `analogWrite()` accepts 0–255 (8-bit). - [ ] Resolution = **$V_{ref} / 1024$** = 4.88 mV (for $V_{ref} = 5$ V). - [ ] LM35 sensitivity = **10 mV/°C**; formula: $T = V_{in} / 0.01$. - [ ] TMP36 has 500 mV offset; formula: $T = 100 \times (V_{in} - 0.5)$. - [ ] HC-SR04 distance: $d = t_{echo} / 58.2$ (in cm). - [ ] Speed of sound ≈ **0.0343 cm/µs** at 20 °C. **PWM & Actuator Driving** - [ ] Duty cycle $D_c = t_{on} / T \times 100\%$. - [ ] Average voltage $V_{avg} = D_c \times V_{CC}$. - [ ] DC motors **require drivers** (L298N, BTS7960, or MOSFET). - [ ] Relays provide **galvanic isolation** for AC/high-voltage loads. - [ ] Servos use 50 Hz PWM with 1–2 ms pulse width for 0°–180°. - [ ] Buzzer: passive vs. active — passive requires tone() with frequency. **Firmware Structure** - [ ] `setup()` runs once; `loop()` repeats indefinitely. - [ ] `pinMode(pin, INPUT/OUTPUT)` is mandatory in `setup()`. - [ ] `INPUT_PULLUP` enables internal pull-up resistor (~20–50 kΩ). - [ ] Use `millis()` instead of `delay()` for non-blocking timing. - [ ] Always include a common GND between Arduino and external power supplies. **Common Sensor Specifications to Memorize** - [ ] DHT11: 0–50 °C, 20–90% RH, ±2 °C accuracy, single-wire digital protocol. - [ ] HC-SR04: 2–400 cm range, 15° cone angle, 40 kHz frequency. - [ ] MQ-2: detects LPG, propane, methane, hydrogen; needs 20 s preheat. - [ ] PIR HC-SR501: 3–7 m range, 120° cone, 5 V logic, retriggerable. **KTU-Specific Favourite Topics** - [ ] Draw the **Arduino sketch structure** (`void setup()`, `void loop()`). - [ ] Explain **ADC** with formula and a numerical example. - [ ] Explain **PWM** generation and its use in LED dimming/motor speed control. - [ ] Justify **why driver ICs are needed** for high-current actuators. - [ ] Solve a numerical problem on LM35 or HC-SR04 with full derivation.

topics.title: Introduction to Raspberry Pi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT612 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Raspberry Pi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Raspberry Pi** is a series of small, low-cost, single-board computers (SBCs) developed by the **Raspberry Pi Foundation** in the United Kingdom, primarily to promote the teaching of basic computer science in schools and developing countries. Architecturally, it is a **System on Chip (SoC)** based development board that integrates a **Broadcom (BCM)** processor, **ARM Cortex** CPU, on-chip **GPU**, **RAM**, and a rich set of I/O peripherals (GPIO, USB, HDMI, Ethernet, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth) onto a single printed circuit board (PCB).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Raspberry Pi is officially categorized as a **Single-Board Computer (SBC)** — *not* a microcontroller. This distinction is a **favourite KTU examiner question** and a common pitfall area (Raspberry Pi = SBC, Arduino = Microcontroller).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you take a fully working desktop computer, remove the bulky metal case, the large cooling fan, the power-hungry power supply, and the spinning hard drive — and shrink the entire motherboard to the size of a **credit card**. That is essentially a Raspberry Pi.

- **The CPU/GPU** is the "brain" that thinks and draws.
- **The GPIO pins** are the "ears, eyes, and hands" that listen to sensors and control motors/LEDs.
- **The microSD card** is the "filing cabinet" that stores the operating system and your files.
- **The HDMI port** is the "mouth" that talks to your TV/monitor.

In the IoT world, the Raspberry Pi acts as the **central intelligence (edge gateway)** that collects sensor data, processes it locally, makes decisions, and pushes it to the cloud — all while sipping only about **5V/3A (15W)** of power.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Operating Voltage:** **5V DC** (via USB-C / Micro-USB)
- **GPIO Logic Level:** **3.3V DC** (NOT 5V tolerant on most pins)
- **Architecture:** **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine)**
- **Default OS:** **Raspberry Pi OS** (formerly Raspbian, Debian-based Linux)
- **Form Factor:** **85.6 mm × 56.5 mm** (credit-card size)

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> The Raspberry Pi was conceived by **Eben Upton** at the **University of Cambridge**, UK. The first model (**Raspberry Pi 1 Model B**) was released on **February 29, 2012**. The name is a nod to the tradition of naming computers after fruits (Apple, Acorn, Apricot) and the mathematical term "Pi".

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Architecture — How It Works

The Raspberry Pi operates on a layered architecture. Let us dissect the operational pipeline:

1. **Power Input Stage:** A **5V DC** supply is fed via USB-C. This is regulated down to lower voltages (1.8V, 3.3V) by onboard voltage regulators to power the SoC and peripherals.
2. **SoC (System on Chip):** The heart is the **Broadcom BCM** chip. It integrates:
   - **CPU** (Central Processing Unit) — executes instructions (ARM Cortex-A series, 64-bit).
   - **GPU** (Graphics Processing Unit) — handles video output via HDMI (VideoCore IV/VI/VII).
   - **RAM** (LPDDR) — soldered on top of the SoC (Package-on-Package).
3. **Boot Sequence:** Power ON → reads **bootcode.bin** from microSD → loads **start.elf** (firmware) → loads **kernel.img** (Linux kernel) → mounts root filesystem → user login prompt.
4. **GPIO Controller:** A separate chip (or part of the SoC) exposes **40-pin header** General Purpose Input/Output lines. Each pin can be configured as input or output under software control.
5. **Peripheral Interfaces:** USB, Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n/ac), Bluetooth (4.2/5.0), CSI (Camera Serial Interface), DSI (Display Serial Interface), and Audio jack.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Raspberry Pi 4 Model B | Raspberry Pi 5 | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SoC** | Broadcom BCM2711 | Broadcom BCM2712 | — |
| **CPU** | Cortex-A72 (Quad-core) | Cortex-A76 (Quad-core) | 64-bit ARM |
| **Clock Speed** | 1.5 / 1.8 | 2.4 | GHz |
| **RAM** | 1 / 2 / 4 / 8 | 4 / 8 | GB LPDDR4/4X |
| **GPU** | VideoCore VI @ 500 MHz | VideoCore VII @ 800 MHz | — |
| **Power Input** | 5V $\vert$ 3.0A | 5V $\vert$ 5.0A (PD) | Volts $\vert$ Amperes |
| **GPIO Voltage** | 3.3 | 3.3 | V (NOT 5V tolerant) |
| **Max GPIO Current** | 16 (per pin), 50 (all) | 16 (per pin), 50 (all) | mA |
| **Wi-Fi** | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 802.11ac |
| **Bluetooth** | 5.0 | 5.0 | BLE |
| **Ethernet** | Gigabit | Gigabit | Mbps |
| **USB** | 2× USB 3.0, 2× USB 2.0 | 2× USB 3.0, 2× USB 2.0 | — |
| **Display** | 2× micro-HDMI (4K) | 2× micro-HDMI (4K) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> The Raspberry Pi is the **Swiss Army Knife of IoT**. It is used as: a Home Media Server (Plex/Kodi), a Network-Attached Storage (NAS) controller, an AI Edge device (running TensorFlow Lite for image recognition), a Retro Gaming Console (RetroPie), a Smart Home Hub (Home Assistant), a VPN Gateway, and a **MQTT broker** for industrial IoT.

### 2.3 Comparison Matrix — Pi vs Arduino (High-Yield for KTU)

| Feature | Raspberry Pi | Arduino Uno |
|---|---|---|
| **Category** | Single-Board Computer (SBC) | Microcontroller Board |
| **Processor** | ARM Cortex (GHz) | ATmega328P (16 MHz) |
| **OS** | Linux (Raspberry Pi OS) | None (bare-metal firmware) |
| **RAM** | 1–8 GB | 2 KB SRAM |
| **Power** | 5V $\vert$ 3A | 5V $\vert$ $\sim$50 mA |
| **GPIO Voltage** | **3.3V** | **5V** |
| **Best For** | Complex tasks, AI, Networking | Real-time, simple control |
| **Cost** | Higher (~$35–$60) | Lower (~$25) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since Raspberry Pi is primarily a hardware description topic, the "derivations" below are the standard engineering calculations students are expected to master for the KTU board exam: **Power budget, GPIO current limiting resistor design, and CPU clock cycle calculations**.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: GPIO Current Limiting Resistor (for LED Interfacing)

**Problem Statement:** You want to connect a red LED (forward voltage $V_f = 2.0\text{ V}$, forward current $I_f = 20\text{ mA}$) to GPIO pin 17 (logic HIGH = 3.3 V) of a Raspberry Pi 4. Calculate the value of the series current-limiting resistor $R$.

**Given Data:**
- Supply voltage (GPIO HIGH) $V_s = 3.3\text{ V}$
- LED forward voltage $V_f = 2.0\text{ V}$
- LED forward current $I_f = 20\text{ mA} = 0.020\text{ A}$

**Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop.**

$$
V_s \;=\; V_R \;+\; V_f
$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to solve for the voltage drop across the resistor $R$.**

$$
V_R \;=\; V_s \;-\; V_f
$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**

$$
V_R \;=\; 3.3\text{ V} \;-\; 2.0\text{ V} \;=\; 1.3\text{ V}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Ohm's Law ($V = IR$) to find $R$.**

$$
R \;=\; \frac{V_R}{I_f}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute and calculate.**

$$
R \;=\; \frac{1.3\text{ V}}{0.020\text{ A}} \;=\; 65\ \Omega
$$

**Step 6: Select the next higher standard E12 resistor value.**

$$
R_{\text{standard}} \;=\; 68\ \Omega
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reasoning:** The 3.3 V GPIO is *not* 5 V tolerant. Connecting an LED without a resistor will exceed the **16 mA per-pin absolute maximum** and permanently damage the Broadcom SoC. A 68 Ω resistor safely limits the current to $\frac{3.3 - 2.0}{68} \approx 19.1\text{ mA}$, which is within the 20 mA target.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Consumption of Raspberry Pi 4

**Problem Statement:** A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B is powered by a **5 V / 3.0 A** USB-C adapter. Calculate the power drawn, the energy consumed over 24 hours, and the yearly electricity cost (assume ₹8 per kWh).

**Step 1: Apply the electrical power equation.**

$$
P \;=\; V \times I
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the rated values.**

$$
P \;=\; 5\text{ V} \times 3.0\text{ A} \;=\; 15\text{ W}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the total energy consumed in 24 hours (in Wh).**

$$
E_{\text{Wh}} \;=\; P \times t \;=\; 15\text{ W} \times 24\text{ h} \;=\; 360\text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to kilowatt-hours (kWh) for billing.**

$$
E_{\text{kWh}} \;=\; \frac{360\text{ Wh}}{1000} \;=\; 0.36\text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate yearly consumption (365 days).**

$$
E_{\text{year}} \;=\; 0.36 \times 365 \;=\; 131.4\text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate the annual cost.**

$$
\text{Cost} \;=\; 131.4 \times 8 \;=\; \text{₹}\ 1051.20\text{ / year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Raspberry Pi 4 consumes roughly the same power as a single LED light bulb — a key reason it is favoured for **always-on IoT edge gateways**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: CPU Clock Cycles per Instruction (CPI)

**Problem Statement:** The Raspberry Pi 4's Cortex-A72 runs at **1.5 GHz**. A custom IoT program executes **2 × 10⁹ instructions**. If the average **Cycles Per Instruction (CPI) = 1.2**, calculate the program execution time.

**Step 1: Relate CPU clock frequency to cycle time.**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} \;=\; \frac{1}{f} \;=\; \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^9\text{ Hz}} \;=\; 0.667\text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate total CPU cycles required.**

$$
\text{Total Cycles} \;=\; \text{Instructions} \times \text{CPI} \;=\; 2 \times 10^9 \times 1.2 \;=\; 2.4 \times 10^9\text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate execution time.**

$$
T_{\text{exec}} \;=\; \text{Total Cycles} \times T_{\text{cycle}} \;=\; 2.4 \times 10^9 \times 0.667 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
T_{\text{exec}} \;=\; 1.6\text{ seconds}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Raspberry Pi

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the internal data, power, and signal flow inside the Raspberry Pi 4 SoC and its peripherals.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PSU[5V DC Power Supply USB-C] --> REG1[3.3V LDO Regulator]
    PSU --> REG2[1.8V LDO Regulator]
    PSU --> REG3[Core Voltage Regulator]
    
    REG1 --> GPIO_C[3.3V GPIO Bank 40 pins]
    REG3 --> SOC[BCM2711 SoC]
    REG2 --> SOC
    
    SOC --> CPU[ARM Cortex-A72 Quad Core CPU 1.5 GHz]
    SOC --> GPU[VideoCore VI GPU 500 MHz]
    SOC --> RAM[LPDDR4 RAM 1 to 8 GB]
    
    CPU --> BUS[Internal AMBA AXI Bus]
    BUS --> GPIO_C
    BUS --> USB_C[USB 3.0 and USB 2.0 Controller]
    BUS --> ETH[Gigabit Ethernet Controller]
    BUS --> WIFIBT[Wi-Fi and Bluetooth 5.0 Module]
    BUS --> HDMI[2x micro HDMI 4K Output]
    BUS --> CSI[CSI Camera Port]
    BUS --> DSI[DSI Display Port]
    BUS --> SDC[MicroSD Card Slot]
    
    GPIO_C --> SENSORS[External Sensors DHT22 Ultrasonic PIR]
    GPIO_C --> ACTUATORS[External Actuators LED Relay Motor]

    classDef power fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef compute fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class PSU,REG1,REG2,REG3 power
    class SOC,CPU,GPU,RAM,BUS compute
    class GPIO_C,USB_C,ETH,WIFIBT,HDMI,CSI,DSI,SDC io
    class SENSORS,ACTUATORS ext
```

### 4.2 GPIO 40-Pin Header Reference Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Top["PIN 1 - 20 (Top Row)"]
        P1[Pin 1: 3.3V]
        P3[Pin 3: GPIO2 I2C SDA]
        P5[Pin 5: GPIO3 I2C SCL]
        P7[Pin 7: GPIO4 GCLK]
        P9[Pin 9: GND]
        P11[Pin 11: GPIO17]
        P13[Pin 13: GPIO27]
        P15[Pin 15: GPIO22]
        P17[Pin 17: 3.3V]
        P19[Pin 19: GPIO10 SPI MOSI]
    end

    subgraph Bot["PIN 2 - 40 (Bottom Row)"]
        P2[Pin 2: 5V]
        P4[Pin 4: 5V]
        P6[Pin 6: GND]

        P10[Pin 10: GPIO15 UART RXD]
        P12[Pin 12: GPIO18 PWM]
```
