```mermaid
        P16[Pin 16: GPIO23]
        P18[Pin 18: GPIO24]
        P20[Pin 20: GND]
    end

    Top ~~~ Bot
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner's Note:** The GPIO header follows the **standard 40-pin layout** introduced from Raspberry Pi Model B+ (2014) onwards. Pin 1 is the **3.3V** pin located closest to the SD card slot. The numbering convention is **odd on the inner row, even on the outer row**.

### 4.3 Typical IoT Application Flow using Raspberry Pi

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Sensors Temperature Humidity Motion] -->|Analog/Digital Signal| B[ADC or Direct GPIO]
    B --> C[Raspberry Pi Processor]
    C --> D{Threshold Check}
    D -->|Yes| E[Activate Actuator Relay Motor]
    D -->|No| F[Log Data to local DB]
    C --> G[Wi-Fi Module]
    G --> H[Cloud Platform AWS IoT ThingSpeak Firebase]
    H --> I[Mobile Dashboard or Web App]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** Raspberry Pi (SBC, 3.3V GPIO, runs Linux) with Arduino (Microcontroller, 5V GPIO, bare-metal). This single confusion costs **2–3 marks** every year.
> 2. **Do NOT write 5V** when asked about GPIO logic level — it is **3.3V**. Applying 5V to a Pi GPIO **permanently destroys** the SoC.
> 3. **Do NOT omit the OS name** — always mention "Raspberry Pi OS (Debian-based Linux)" for full credit.
> 4. When asked for "applications," do **not** list vague answers like "IoT." Be specific: *home automation gateway, AI edge inference, MQTT broker, media server*.

---

### 📘 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Define the term Single-Board Computer (SBC). How is the Raspberry Pi classified as an SBC?**

**Model Answer:**
A Single-Board Computer (SBC) is a complete computer built on a single printed circuit board, integrating the microprocessor (CPU), memory (RAM), input/output (I/O) ports, and other essential components — without needing expansion cards.
The **Raspberry Pi** qualifies as an SBC because its **Broadcom BCM2711 SoC** integrates the ARM Cortex-A72 CPU, VideoCore VI GPU, LPDDR4 RAM, GPIO header, USB, HDMI, Ethernet, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth all on a single PCB of credit-card size (85.6 mm × 56.5 mm). It runs a full operating system (Raspberry Pi OS), unlike a microcontroller.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Expected]**
**List any six key features/specifications of the Raspberry Pi 4 Model B.**

**Model Answer (Any 6):**
1. **SoC:** Broadcom BCM2711
2. **CPU:** Quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 @ 1.5 GHz (64-bit)
3. **GPU:** VideoCore VI @ 500 MHz, supports OpenGL ES 3.0
4. **RAM:** 1/2/4/8 GB LPDDR4
5. **Connectivity:** Dual-band (2.4/5 GHz) Wi-Fi, Bluetooth 5.0, Gigabit Ethernet
6. **USB:** 2× USB 3.0 + 2× USB 2.0
7. **Display:** 2× micro-HDMI supporting 4K @ 60 fps
8. **GPIO:** 40-pin header (3.3V logic)
9. **Power:** 5V/3A via USB-C
10. **Storage:** microSD card slot

---

### 📗 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Internal Choice Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme mandates that Module 3 offers internal choice. **Answer ANY ONE** of the following.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture & Pin Analysis**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of the Raspberry Pi 4 Model B**. Identify the role of the **SoC, CPU, GPU, RAM, and GPIO controller**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **40-pin GPIO header** of the Raspberry Pi in detail. List at least **three special-function pins** (I2C, SPI, UART) with their pin numbers. Why is it important to use a **current-limiting resistor** when interfacing an LED? Calculate the resistor value for a red LED ($V_f = 2.0\text{ V}$, $I_f = 15\text{ mA}$) connected to a 3.3 V GPIO pin. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Internal Architecture (7 Marks):**
The Raspberry Pi 4 architecture is centred on the **Broadcom BCM2711 SoC**, which integrates the CPU, GPU, and RAM interface. The **ARM Cortex-A72 quad-core CPU** (1.5 GHz) handles general-purpose computation and runs the **Raspberry Pi OS (Linux kernel)**. The **VideoCore VI GPU** at 500 MHz drives the dual micro-HDMI ports and supports H.265 4K video decode. The **LPDDR4 RAM** (1–8 GB) is mounted using **Package-on-Package (PoP)** technology directly above the SoC. The **GPIO controller** is part of the SoC and exposes 28 usable GPIO lines over the 40-pin header, each operating at **3.3V logic**. Peripherals like USB 3.0, Gigabit Ethernet, Wi-Fi/Bluetooth (via the Cypress CYW43455 chip), CSI camera, and DSI display are connected through the **AMBA AXI bus**. Power is supplied via 5V USB-C and regulated on-board to 3.3V, 1.8V, and core voltages.

*Examiner allocation: 1 mark for SoC, 1 for CPU, 1 for GPU, 1 for RAM, 1 for GPIO, 1 for peripherals, 1 for power/bus.*

**(b) GPIO Header & LED Resistor (7 Marks):**
The 40-pin GPIO header is the **standard layout** introduced from Model B+. It contains:
- **Power pins:** 5V (Pins 2, 4), 3.3V (Pins 1, 17), GND (Pins 6, 9, 14, 20, 25, 30, 34, 39).
- **GPIO pins:** 26 general-purpose pins (default GPIO2–GPIO27).
- **Special-function pins:**
  - **I2C:** SDA = GPIO2 (Pin 3), SCL = GPIO3 (Pin 5)
  - **SPI:** MOSI = GPIO10 (Pin 19), MISO = GPIO9 (Pin 21), SCLK = GPIO11 (Pin 23), CE0 = GPIO8 (Pin 24)
  - **UART:** TXD = GPIO14 (Pin 8), RXD = GPIO15 (Pin 10)
  - **PWM:** GPIO12, GPIO13, GPIO18, GPIO19

A **current-limiting resistor** is **mandatory** because the Pi GPIO can source/sink only **16 mA per pin** (absolute max). Exceeding this permanently burns the BCM chip.

**Calculation:**

$$
V_R \;=\; V_s - V_f \;=\; 3.3 - 2.0 \;=\; 1.3\text{ V}
$$

$$
R \;=\; \frac{V_R}{I_f} \;=\; \frac{1.3}{0.015} \;=\; 86.67\ \Omega
$$

$$
R_{\text{standard}} \;=\; 100\ \Omega \quad (\text{next higher E12 value})
$$

*Examiner allocation: 2 marks for pin list, 2 marks for special-function pins, 1 mark for resistor importance, 2 marks for calculation.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Comparison, OS, and IoT Applications**
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Raspberry Pi and Arduino Uno** based on: processor type, operating system, clock speed, RAM, GPIO voltage, and application suitability. State **two reasons** why Raspberry Pi is preferred for IoT gateway applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure to set up a Raspberry Pi** as an IoT node. List any **five real-world IoT applications** where the Raspberry Pi is used. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pi vs Arduino (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Raspberry Pi 4 | Arduino Uno |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Single-Board Computer (SBC) | Microcontroller Board |
| **Processor** | ARM Cortex-A72 (64-bit) | ATmega328P (8-bit AVR) |
| **Clock Speed** | 1.5 GHz | 16 MHz |
| **OS** | Raspberry Pi OS (Linux) | None (bare-metal) |
| **RAM** | 1–8 GB LPDDR4 | 2 KB SRAM + 32 KB Flash |
| **GPIO Voltage** | 3.3 V | 5 V |
| **Connectivity** | Built-in Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, Ethernet | Requires shields (ESP8266) |
| **Best Suited For** | Complex tasks, networking, AI | Real-time, simple control |

**Two reasons Pi is preferred for IoT gateway:**
1. **Full OS support** allows running **MQTT brokers, Node-RED, Docker containers, and Python scripts** simultaneously — impossible on an Arduino.
2. **Built-in networking** (Wi-Fi, Ethernet, Bluetooth) eliminates the need for external shields, making it a self-sufficient edge gateway.

*Examiner allocation: 1 mark per correct row × 5 rows = 5 marks, 2 marks for IoT reasons.*

**(b) Setup Procedure & Applications (7 Marks):**

**Step-by-step setup of Raspberry Pi for IoT:**
1. **Hardware assembly:** Insert microSD card (≥16 GB, Class 10), connect HDMI to monitor, plug in USB keyboard/mouse, connect 5V/3A USB-C adapter.
2. **OS flashing:** Download **Raspberry Pi Imager** on a PC. Select "Raspberry Pi OS (64-bit)", choose the SD card, and click **Write**.
3. **First boot:** The Pi boots into the desktop. Configure Wi-Fi, set locale, and enable SSH via `sudo raspi-config`.
4. **Update packages:** Run `sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y` in the terminal.
5. **Install IoT libraries:** `pip3 install paho-mqtt, RPi.GPIO, Adafruit_DHT` for sensor and MQTT integration.
6. **Connect sensors:** Wire DHT22 (temperature/humidity) to GPIO4 with a 10 kΩ pull-up resistor.
7. **Deploy code:** Write a Python script to read sensor data and publish it to the **ThingSpeak / AWS IoT Core / Adafruit IO** cloud via MQTT.

**Five real-world IoT applications:**
1. **Smart Home Hub** — Home Assistant open-source automation.
2. **Weather Station** — DHT22 + BMP180 sensors uploading to ThingSpeak.
3. **AI Edge Camera** — Raspberry Pi Camera Module V3 + TensorFlow Lite for person detection.
4. **Industrial MQTT Broker** — Running Mosquitto for factory floor sensor data.
5. **Smart Agriculture** — Soil moisture + pH sensors controlling irrigation valves via GPIO relays.

*Examiner allocation: 1 mark per setup step (first 5 steps = 5 marks) + 2 marks for applications list.*

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Raspberry Pi = Single-Board Computer (SBC)**, **not a microcontroller**. Runs a full **Linux OS**.
- ✅ **Inventor:** Eben Upton; **First release:** February 2012; **Foundation:** Raspberry Pi Foundation, UK.
- ✅ **SoC:** Broadcom BCM2711 (Pi 4) / BCM2712 (Pi 5); **CPU:** ARM Cortex (64-bit).
- ✅ **GPIO logic level is 3.3V — NEVER 5V.** Max current per pin = **16 mA**.
- ✅ **Standard OS:** Raspberry Pi OS (Debian-based, formerly Raspbian).
- ✅ **Power input:** 5V via USB-C (Pi 4: 3A, Pi 5: 5A PD).
- ✅ **40-pin GPIO header** is **standard from Model B+** (2014).
- ✅ **Special pins:** I2C (GPIO2/3), SPI (GPIO9/10/11/8), UART (GPIO14/15), PWM (GPIO12/13/18/19).
- ✅ **Applications:** Home automation, AI edge inference, MQTT broker, weather station, media server, retro gaming, VPN, NAS.
- ✅ **Pi vs Arduino:** Pi = powerful, networked, complex tasks. Arduino = real-time, low-power, simple control.
- ✅ **Boot sequence:** bootcode.bin → start.elf → kernel.img → rootfs (from microSD).
- ✅ **Form factor:** 85.6 mm × 56.5 mm (credit-card size).
- ✅ **Built-in wireless:** Wi-Fi (2.4/5 GHz) + Bluetooth 5.0 (from Pi 3 onwards).
- ✅ **LED resistor formula:** $R = \frac{V_s - V_f}{I_f}$; always choose the next **higher** E12 standard value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation of IoT with Raspberry Pi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT612 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Implementation of IoT with Raspberry Pi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Raspberry Pi** is a series of small, single-board computers (SBCs) developed by the **Raspberry Pi Foundation** (UK) primarily to promote teaching of basic computer science in schools and developing countries. In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)**, the Raspberry Pi acts as a **low-cost, low-power, edge-computing gateway or end-node device** capable of sensing the physical environment, processing data locally, and communicating with cloud platforms over standard network protocols.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):** Implementation of IoT with Raspberry Pi involves the integration of hardware components (sensors, actuators, GPIO pins), software stack (Raspbian/Raspberry Pi OS, Python libraries), and communication protocols (MQTT, HTTP, CoAP) to build functional IoT systems for real-world applications.

### Key Terminology Table

| Term | Definition | Relevance in KTU Exam |
|------|------------|----------------------|
| **SBC** | Single Board Computer — entire system on one PCB | Foundation architecture |
| **GPIO** | General Purpose Input/Output pins on Pi | Interfacing sensors/actuators |
| **SoC** | System on Chip (Broadcom BCM2837/2711) | Heart of Raspberry Pi |
| **Edge Computing** | Processing data at/near source instead of cloud | Modern IoT paradigm |
| **HAT** | Hardware Attached on Top — Pi add-on boards | Modular expansion |
| **SoC** | System on Chip integrating CPU, GPU, RAM | Raspberry Pi architecture |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Home Brain"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Raspberry Pi as the "Brain of a Smart Home":**

Imagine your house has many **senses** (sensors for temperature, motion, light) and many **muscles** (LEDs, fans, motors). Without a brain, these senses and muscles cannot coordinate. The **Raspberry Pi is that brain**:

- 🧠 **Senses (Inputs)** → Sensors connected via **GPIO pins** (like nerves)
- 💪 **Muscles (Outputs)** → Actuators (LEDs, relays) controlled by **GPIO pins**
- 🗣️ **Mouth (Communication)** → Wi-Fi/Ethernet to talk to the cloud (like sending messages to your phone)
- 🧮 **Memory & Logic** → Python programs running on the **ARM processor** (the cerebral cortex)

When a temperature sensor detects >30°C, the Pi decides to turn on a fan **automatically** — just as your brain decides to sweat when it's hot. This is **edge intelligence** in action!

## 1.3 Why Raspberry Pi for IoT?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Examiners often ask "Why Raspberry Pi over Arduino/Microcontrollers for IoT?" Remember these **4 key advantages**:
> 1. **Full OS support** (Linux) → enables complex applications
> 2. **Built-in networking** (Wi-Fi/Ethernet) → cloud-ready
> 3. **High processing power** (quad-core ARM Cortex) → can run ML models
> 4. **Python ecosystem** → rapid prototyping with rich libraries

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Operating Voltage:** **3.3V** (GPIO logic level) and **5V** (power rail)
- **GPIO Current:** **16mA per pin** (max), **50mA total** across all pins
- **Processor Clock:** **1.5 GHz – 1.8 GHz** (ARM Cortex-A72 in Pi 4)
- **RAM:** **1GB / 2GB / 4GB / 8GB** LPDDR4 (Pi 4 variants)
- **Power Input:** **5V / 3A** via USB-C (Pi 4)
- **GPIO Pins:** **40-pin** header (26 usable GPIO + power/ground)
- **Storage:** **microSD card** (boot + filesystem)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Raspberry Pi Architecture (Block-Level Breakdown)

The Raspberry Pi architecture can be decomposed into **5 functional layers**:

### Layer 1: Hardware Layer (Physical Components)
- **SoC (System on Chip):** Broadcom BCM2711 (Pi 4) or BCM2837 (Pi 3)
- **CPU:** Quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 (64-bit)
- **GPU:** VideoCore VI (handles HDMI output)
- **RAM:** LPDDR4 shared with GPU
- **Connectivity:** Wi-Fi 802.11ac, Bluetooth 5.0, Gigabit Ethernet
- **Ports:** 2× USB 3.0, 2× USB 2.0, HDMI ×2, 3.5mm audio, GPIO header

### Layer 2: GPIO Interfacing Layer
The **40-pin GPIO header** is the bridge between the digital world of software and the physical world of sensors/actuators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin Numbering Convention (CRITICAL for exams):**
> - **BOARD mode:** Numbers pins 1–40 by physical position
> - **BCM mode:** Numbers pins by Broadcom SoC channel (e.g., GPIO17, GPIO27)
> - Always specify which mode you use in code!

### Layer 3: Operating System Layer
- **Raspberry Pi OS** (formerly Raspbian) — Debian-based Linux
- Boots from **microSD card** (typically 16GB+ Class 10)
- Supports **Python 3** as default scripting language

### Layer 4: Middleware/Protocol Layer
- **MQTT** (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) — lightweight pub/sub
- **HTTP/HTTPS** — RESTful APIs
- **CoAP** (Constrained Application Protocol) — UDP-based
- **WebSockets** — full-duplex communication

### Layer 5: Application Layer
- User-written **Python scripts** using libraries like `RPi.GPIO`, `gpiozero`, `paho-mqtt`, `requests`
- Cloud platforms: **AWS IoT, Google Cloud IoT, ThingSpeak, Blynk**

## 2.2 Core Operational Steps in IoT Implementation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6-Step IoT Implementation Pipeline (Raspberry Pi Edition):**

1. **Step 1 — Hardware Setup:** Connect sensors/actuators to GPIO pins via breadboard and jumper wires.
2. **Step 2 — OS Installation:** Flash Raspberry Pi OS onto microSD using Raspberry Pi Imager.
3. **Step 3 — Library Installation:** Install Python libraries (`pip install RPi.GPIO paho-mqtt`).
4. **Step 4 — Code Development:** Write Python script for sensing, processing, and communication.
5. **Step 5 — Network Configuration:** Connect to Wi-Fi and configure MQTT broker/cloud credentials.
6. **Step 6 — Deployment & Monitoring:** Run script as a service using `systemd` or `cron` for 24/7 operation.

## 2.3 GPIO Pin Classification

| Pin Type | Number of Pins | Function | Voltage Level |
|----------|----------------|----------|---------------|
| **Power (3.3V)** | 2 | Supply 3.3V to external components | 3.3V |
| **Power (5V)** | 2 | Supply 5V (direct from USB) | 5V |
| **Ground (GND)** | 8 | Reference ground | 0V |
| **GPIO (General)** | 26 | Digital I/O (configurable) | 3.3V logic |
| **I2C (SDA, SCL)** | 2 (GPIO2, GPIO3) | I2C bus for sensors | 3.3V |
| **SPI (MOSI, MISO, SCLK, CE)** | 4 (GPIO9–11, GPIO8) | SPI bus for fast peripherals | 3.3V |
| **UART (TX, RX)** | 2 (GPIO14, GPIO15) | Serial communication | 3.3V |
| **PCM/PWM** | Configurable | Audio/Pulse Width Modulation | 3.3V |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often connect 5V sensors directly to GPIO pins. **Raspberry Pi GPIO is NOT 5V tolerant** — connecting >3.3V will permanently damage the SoC! Always use a **level shifter** or **voltage divider**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| **Power Consumption** | $P = V \times I$ | Power drawn by Pi | Watts (W) |
| **GPIO Current Limit** | $I_{pin} \leq 16\text{mA}$ | Max current per pin | mA |
| **Total GPIO Current** | $\sum I_{pins} \leq 50\text{mA}$ | Sum across all GPIO | mA |
| **Pull-up Resistance** | $V_{out} = V_{cc} \times \dfrac{R_p}{R_p + R_s}$ | Voltage divider (sensor with pull-up) | Volts |
| **ADC Resolution (10-bit)** | $V_{analog} = \dfrac{ADC\_value}{1024} \times V_{ref}$ | Pi lacks built-in ADC; uses external MCP3008 | Volts |
| **PWM Duty Cycle** | $D = \dfrac{t_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of HIGH time in one cycle | % |
| **MQTT Topic Format** | `device/sensor1/temperature` | Hierarchical topic structure | String |
| **Power Budget** | $P_{total} = P_{Pi} + P_{sensors} + P_{actuators}$ | Total system power | Watts |
| **Heat Dissipation** | $Q = m \times c \times \Delta T$ | Thermal management (advanced) | Joules |
| **Data Rate (SPI)** | $f_{SCLK} = \dfrac{f_{core}}{divisor}$ | SPI clock derivation | Hz |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Applications of Raspberry Pi in IoT:**
> - **Smart Agriculture:** Soil moisture sensing + automated irrigation (uses Pi + MQTT + AWS)
> - **Home Automation:** Voice-controlled lighting (Pi + Google Assistant + relays)
> - **Environmental Monitoring:** Air quality stations deployed in cities (Pi + gas sensors + ThingSpeak)
> - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Predictive maintenance using vibration sensors + ML on Pi
> - **Healthcare:** Remote patient monitoring (Pi + MAX30100 heart rate sensor + Telegram bot)

## 2.6 Communication Protocols Comparison

| Protocol | Transport | Header Size | Best For | Pi Implementation |
|----------|-----------|-------------|----------|-------------------|
| **HTTP/HTTPS** | TCP | Large | RESTful APIs, web dashboards | `requests` library |
| **MQTT** | TCP | 2 bytes | IoT pub/sub, low bandwidth | `paho-mqtt` |
| **CoAP** | UDP | 4 bytes | Constrained devices | `aiocoap` library |
| **WebSocket** | TCP | 2–14 bytes | Real-time bidirectional | `websockets` library |
| **LoRaWAN** | Radio | Variable | Long-range, low power | Pi + LoRa HAT |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete IoT Implementation: Temperature Monitoring System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End-to-End Case Study:** A Raspberry Pi reads temperature data from a **DS18B20** sensor, publishes it via **MQTT** to a cloud broker, and triggers a **buzzer** when temperature exceeds a threshold. This is a classic KTU 14-mark question!

### 3.1.1 Hardware Connection Logic

**DS18B20 Temperature Sensor Wiring:**
- VCC (Red) → Pin 1 (3.3V)
- GND (Black) → Pin 6 (GND)
- DATA (Yellow) → GPIO4 (Pin 7) with **4.7kΩ pull-up resistor** to 3.3V

**Buzzer Wiring:**
- Positive → GPIO17 (Pin 11)
- Negative → GND (Pin 9)

### 3.1.2 Mathematical Justification for Pull-up Resistor

The DS18B20 uses **1-Wire protocol**, which requires a pull-up resistor. Why 4.7kΩ?

$$
R_{pull-up} = \dfrac{V_{cc} - V_{OL}}{I_{OL}}
$$

Where:
- $V_{cc} = 3.3\text{V}$ (Pi logic voltage)
- $V_{OL} = 0.4\text{V}$ (max low output voltage of DS18B20)
- $I_{OL} = 1\text{mA}$ (typical sink current)

$$
R_{pull-up} = \dfrac{3.3 - 0.4}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{2.9}{0.001} = 2900\,\Omega = 2.9\text{k}\Omega
$$

We use **4.7kΩ** (closest standard value) to limit current safely while ensuring fast rise times.

### 3.1.3 Threshold Logic for Buzzer Activation

The decision logic for the buzzer:

$$
\text{Buzzer State} = \begin{cases} \text{ON} & \text{if } T_{current} \geq T_{threshold} \\ \text{OFF} & \text{if } T_{current} < T_{threshold} \end{cases}
$$

Where $T_{threshold} = 30°C$ (configurable). To avoid rapid toggling (chattering), we add **hysteresis**:

$$
T_{hysteresis} = T_{threshold} + 2°C
$$

$$
\text{Buzzer State} = \begin{cases} \text{ON} & \text{if } T_{current} \geq T_{threshold} \\ \text{OFF} & \text{if } T_{current} \leq T_{threshold} - 2°C \\ \text{Hold} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### 3.1.4 Complete Python Implementation

```python
# IoT Temperature Monitoring with Raspberry Pi
# This is the FULL KTU Board-expected code structure

import paho.mqtt.client as mqtt
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time
import json

# ---------- CONFIGURATION ----------
BROKER = "test.mosquitto.org"   # Public MQTT broker
PORT = 1883
TOPIC = "ktu/iot/room1/temp"
BUZZER_PIN = 17                  # GPIO17
TEMP_THRESHOLD = 30.0            # Celsius
HYSTERESIS = 2.0
SENSOR_INTERVAL = 5              # seconds

# ---------- GPIO SETUP ----------
GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setwarnings(False)
GPIO.setup(BUZZER_PIN, GPIO.OUT)

# ---------- MOCK SENSOR READING ----------
# In real exam, use: from w1thermsensor import W1ThermSensor
#                   sensor = W1ThermSensor()
#                   temp_c = sensor.get_temperature()

def read_temperature():
    # Replace with actual DS18B20 read via 1-Wire
    # For demonstration:
    import random
    return round(20 + random.uniform(0, 15), 2)

# ---------- MQTT SETUP ----------
client = mqtt.Client(client_id="RPi_KTU_001")
client.connect(BROKER, PORT, keepalive=60)
client.loop_start()

# ---------- MAIN LOOP ----------
buzzer_state = False
print("[KTU IoT System Started]")

try:
    while True:
        temp = read_temperature()
        payload = json.dumps({
            "device": "RPi-Node-01",
            "temperature": temp,
            "unit": "C",
            "timestamp": time.time()
        })

        # Publish to MQTT
        client.publish(TOPIC, payload, qos=1)
        print(f"Published: {payload}")

        # Threshold logic with hysteresis
        if temp >= TEMP_THRESHOLD and not buzzer_state:
            GPIO.output(BUZZER_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)
            buzzer_state = True
            print(f"[ALERT] Temperature {temp}°C exceeds threshold!")
        elif temp <= (TEMP_THRESHOLD - HYSTERESIS) and buzzer_state:
            GPIO.output(BUZZER_PIN, GPIO.LOW)
            buzzer_state = False
            print(f"[OK] Temperature {temp}°C normalized.")

        time.sleep(SENSOR_INTERVAL)

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("\n[System Shutdown]")
    GPIO.cleanup()
    client.loop_stop()
    client.disconnect()
```

### 3.1.5 Step-by-Step Code Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Line-by-line breakdown for KTU answer writing:**

| Line | Code Element | Explanation |
|------|--------------|-------------|
| 1–5 | `import` statements | Import MQTT client, GPIO library, JSON encoder, time module |
| 7–12 | Configuration constants | Define broker, port, topic, pin, threshold (uncomment and modify for different scenarios) |
| 14–16 | GPIO initialization | Set BCM mode, disable warnings, configure pin as OUTPUT |
| 18–22 | Sensor function | Reads temperature (in exam, mention `w1thermsensor` library for DS18B20) |
| 24–25 | MQTT client | Connect to public broker, start background loop |
| 27–30 | Main loop | Continuously read, publish, decide |
| 32–36 | JSON payload | Structured data with metadata (best practice) |
| 38–44 | Hysteresis control | Prevents buzzer from rapid on/off (industrial logic) |
| 46–50 | Cleanup | Proper resource release on Ctrl+C |

## 3.2 Data Flow Derivation — From Sensor to Cloud

$$
\text{Sensor} \xrightarrow{\text{Analog/Digital}} \text{GPIO} \xrightarrow{\text{Interrupt/Polling}} \text{Python Script} \xrightarrow{\text{JSON}} \text{MQTT Client} \xrightarrow{\text{TCP/IP}} \text{Broker} \xrightarrow{\text{Topic Subscribe}} \text{Cloud Dashboard}
$$

### Latency Calculation (Optional Advanced Topic)

For a typical MQTT publish:

$$
t_{total} = t_{sense} + t_{process} + t_{publish} + t_{network}
$$

$$
t_{total} = 50\text{ms} + 10\text{ms} + 5\text{ms} + 100\text{ms} = 165\text{ms}
$$

This **165ms** end-to-end latency is acceptable for **most IoT applications** (industrial control often requires <1s).

## 3.3 Power Budget Derivation

For a Raspberry Pi 4 system with peripherals:

$$
P_{Pi} = 5\text{V} \times 3\text{A} = 15\text{W}
$$

$$
P_{sensor} = 3.3\text{V} \times 5\text{mA} = 0.0165\text{W}
$$

$$
P_{buzzer} = 5\text{V} \times 30\text{mA} = 0.15\text{W}
$$

$$
P_{total} = 15 + 0.0165 + 0.15 \approx 15.17\text{W}
$$

This justifies using a **5V/3A power supply** with at least 20% headroom.
